body: Password Requirements When the complex password policy is enabled, all user passwords must meet the following criteria: Requirement Detail Minimum length 8 characters Name restriction Cannot contain the user’s account name or parts of their full name exceeding two consecutive characters Password history Cannot match any of the last 10 passwords Expiry Must be changed every 90 days Storage & transmission Must not be displayed, stored, or transmitted in clear text § Character Complexity Your password must include characters from any three of the four categories listed below — you do not need to use all four: English uppercase characters (A through Z) English lowercase characters (a through z) Base 10 digits (0 through 9) Non-alphabetic characters (e.g. ! , $ , # , % ) i Tip: A strong password example is Blue$ky92 — it draws from all four character categories. The diagram below shows which character belongs to which category: B l u e $ k y 9 2 UPPERCASE LOWERCASE SPECIAL LOWERCASE DIGITS All 4 categories used — only 3 are required Uppercase Lowercase Digit Special

Logging In When a user attempts to log in, they will be directed to the Password Reset screen if either of the following conditions is met: 90 days have elapsed since their last password change. They click the "Forgot your password?" link and then follow the reset link sent to their email. RapidReconciler login screen The "Forgot your password?" link sits below the password field. Clicking it sends a password reset link to the email address on file for the user. i Note: The password reset link sent by email is time-limited. If the link has expired, the user should repeat the "Forgot your password?" process to receive a new link. If the issue persists, contact your RapidReconciler administrator.

Resetting a Password On the Password Reset screen, the new password must conform to the policy outlined above. The Confirm button remains disabled until the password meets the 8-character minimum length requirement. Password Reset screen Enter a new password in both fields. The Confirm button enables once the 8-character minimum is met. Common reasons a new password may be rejected: The password is fewer than 8 characters The password does not meet the three-category complexity requirement The password contains the user’s name or account name The password matches one of the last 10 previously used passwords When a complexity rule is violated, an error banner appears below the Confirm button explaining what is missing: Complexity error banner Fires when the password meets the 8-character minimum but uses fewer than three of the four character categories. To resolve, add a missing category — for example, a digit or symbol. If your password is being rejected and you are unsure why, review the requirements above and try a different combination of characters.

Administration § Assigning a Temporary Password The RapidReconciler administrator has the ability to assign a temporary password to a new user. Upon first login, the user will be required to reset the temporary password before gaining access to the application. For step-by-step instructions on creating users and assigning passwords, see Administrator Walkthrough → User Management ! Best Practice: When assigning a temporary password, notify the user through a separate communication channel (e.g. phone or a separate email) rather than including the password in the same message as the login instructions. This reduces the risk of unauthorized access. § Enabling or Disabling the Password Policy The complex password policy can be enabled or disabled at the application level by GSI support. When disabled, users are not subject to the complexity, history, or expiry requirements described in this document. See Enabling the Complex Password Policy below for the activation request process. ! Recommendation: It is strongly recommended to keep the complex password policy enabled in all production environments to protect access to sensitive financial data. § Locked or Inaccessible Accounts If a user is unable to log in and cannot complete the password reset process — for example, they no longer have access to the email address on file — the administrator can assign a new temporary password directly from the Admin > Users page in RapidReconciler. See Administrator Walkthrough → User Management for instructions on editing a user and assigning a new password

Enabling the Complex Password Policy The complex password policy is enabled at the application level by GSI support. To request activation for your RapidReconciler environment, send an email to rrsupport@getgsi.com with the following information: Your company name and RapidReconciler account details The name of your RapidReconciler administrator Your requested activation date i Note: It is recommended to notify all users in advance before the policy is enabled. Existing users will be required to reset their passwords at their next login once the policy is active.

Quick Reference Scenario Action Password expired A message box appears stating that the password has expired, with a button to reset the password Forgot password Click "Forgot your password?" and follow the emailed reset link New user first login Reset the temporary password assigned by the administrator Password reset link expired Repeat the "Forgot your password?" process Account inaccessible Contact your RapidReconciler administrator to receive a new temporary password ← Back to Getting Started Need help? rrsupport@getgsi.com

Inventory Offsets Pre-configure the GL accounts that should be used to offset End of Day and Transaction variances. When set up, these accounts and their associated amounts are populated automatically in the Suggested Entry popup that opens when a user clicks the star icon on a variance category row in the Variance Calculation section of the Reconciliation page. Navigation Offset account settings are accessed by navigating to Admin > Inventory Offsets in the main navigation panel. Admin > Inventory Offsets The setup grid for offset accounts. Each row is a unique combination of Company, Variance Source, Document Type, and Order Type. What Inventory Offsets Do Offset accounts work for two of the variance sources on the Reconciliation page: End of Day and Transactions . The grid shown above lists every combination of Company, Document Type, and Order Type that appears in the applicable variance reports. The administrator enters the GL account that should offset the variance for each combination. The result: when a user clicks the star icon next to End of Day or Transactions in the Variance Calculation section of the Reconciliation page, the Suggested Entry popup opens with the pre-configured offset accounts and their associated amounts already populated — saving the user from having to look up and enter the offset accounts manually each period. Where the Star Icon Appears The star icon appears next to applicable rows in the Variance Calculation section of the Reconciliation page: VARIANCE CALCULATION SOURCE VARIANCE ACTION End of Day (2,847.50) Click to open Suggested Entry Transactions 125.00 Roll Forward 0.00 — Star icon on the Reconciliation page The star icon appears only on rows where offset accounts have been pre-configured in Admin > Inventory Offsets — End of Day and Transactions. The Suggested Entry Popup Clicking the star opens the Suggested Entry popup. The offset accounts and amounts you configure in Admin > Inventory Offsets are populated automatically — the user simply reviews the entry and uses it to drive the closing journal entry in JD Edwards: Suggested Entry — End of Day Variance Company: 00010 Acme, Inc. · Period: 12/2025 DOCTYPE ORDER TYPE OFFSET ACCOUNT DEBIT CREDIT SO SO 1.5020.INVADJ 1,425.00 — IA — 1.5025.INVADJ — 487.50 PI — 1.5030.INVADJ 935.00 — TOTAL 2,360.00 487.50 Net Variance: (1,872.50) Cancel Use Entry Suggested Entry popup With offset accounts pre-configured, the popup opens fully populated. The user reviews the entry and clicks Use Entry to confirm — no manual lookup of GL accounts required. Grid Columns Column Description Company The JD Edwards company number the offset applies to. VarSource The variance source — EOD (End of Day) or TRX (Transactions). DocType The JDE document type for the variance row. OrderType The JDE order type for the variance row. Offset Account The GL account the administrator assigns to offset the variance amount for that combination. Filtering the Grid Two dropdowns above the grid help narrow what is displayed: Filter Use Source Restrict the grid to a single variance source — End of Day or Transactions — or show All rows together. Offset Filter by whether an offset account has been assigned, allowing administrators to quickly find rows still needing configuration. Configuration Workflow Navigate to Admin > Inventory Offsets . Use the Source and Offset dropdowns to filter to rows that still need an account assigned. For each Company / DocType / OrderType combination, enter the appropriate GL offset account. Save changes. The configured accounts will appear in the Suggested Entry popup the next time a user clicks the star icon next to End of Day or Transactions on the Reconciliation page. Freeform field The offset account input is freeform — RapidReconciler does not validate the value against JD Edwards. This is intentional: it allows administrators to enter generic account names (for example, TBD , Inventory Variance , or any internally meaningful label) and let internal procedures handle the actual GL mapping when the entry is posted in JDE. When to use this Configuring offset accounts is most valuable in environments that close multiple companies or generate many variance rows each period. Pre-assigning the accounts means the Suggested Entry popup is mostly built for the user — they simply review and use the entry to drive the closing JE.

Company Management Configure licensed companies, fiscal start dates, currency settings, AAI documents, and reconciliation thresholds for each company in the application. Navigation Company settings are accessed by navigating to Admin > Companies in the main navigation panel. Administrator rights must be assigned to your RapidReconciler user ID in order for this option to be visible. Clicking the Companies link displays a list of all licensed companies. To modify a company's settings, click the Options icon in the far-right column of the applicable row. Licensed Companies The Companies page displays all companies currently licensed for use in RapidReconciler. The following fields are shown for each company: Admin > Companies The Licensed Companies grid. Each row is one licensed company; the icons on the right open the Options popup or trigger a Re-roll. Field Description Number The company number as defined in JD Edwards. Name The company name. Start Date The earliest fiscal period available for reconciliation. Base Currency The base currency of the company, pulled from JD Edwards. Report Currency The currency used for reporting purposes. Rate Type The exchange rate type used for currency conversion, if applicable. AAI Doc The AAI document type used for the model DMAAI table. Threshold The reconciliation threshold value, expressed in the company's base currency. Variances at or below this absolute value are treated as within tolerance and flagged accordingly on the Reconciliation page. Options Click to open the Company Options pop-up for editing. Reroll Click to reroll the company, which recalculates the perpetual balance from the baseline date forward. Typically used if transactions have been backdated more than 1 period. Important Only GSI can add or remove companies, as they are managed per license agreement. If additional company licenses are required, please contact GSI at gsisales@getgsi.com . Reroll by Company The Reroll icon on each company row recalculates the perpetual inventory balance from the baseline forward. Run it when an end user reports an inventory carry-forward issue — for example, the Inventory Validation light is red and the report shows VarOK = No while GLOK = Yes . What happens when you click it: A confirmation pop-up asks “Are you sure?” Click OK and let the process run to completion. For each inventory account on the company, RapidReconciler starts at the beginning perpetual balance and rolls forward by adding the cardex transaction amounts period by period. It is a clean recalculation, so it resolves carry-forward problems caused by backdated transactions or transient system glitches that left a stale value in place. See the Inventory validation light is red scenario for the end-user perspective on when to request a reroll. Company Options Clicking the Options icon opens the Company Options pop-up window: Company Options Popup The Company Number and Base Currency fields appear with a grey background to indicate they are read-only; they are pulled directly from JD Edwards. The Company Number and Base Currency fields are read-only, as they are pulled directly from JD Edwards and cannot be changed within RapidReconciler. The following fields may be modified: Start Date The Start Date represents the earliest fiscal period that can be reconciled. It is always set to the first day of a fiscal period — typically the first day of the current fiscal year. Once the current fiscal year is closed, the administrator should update this date to reflect the new fiscal year. Irreversible action The Start Date cannot be moved backwards . Once advanced, RapidReconciler will initiate a purge procedure to permanently remove historical data prior to the new date. Verify your fiscal year is fully closed and reconciled before advancing this date. Key considerations It is highly recommended that at least 2 months of history be retained in the RapidReconciler database at all times. Advancing the Start Date is a method of recovering server resources and improving application performance. Reducing the volume of data in the database results in faster response times. Report Currency The currency used for reporting. This may differ from the base currency where multi-currency reporting is required. Rate Type The exchange rate type to be applied for currency conversions, where applicable. AAI Doc The AAI document type used to identify the model DMAAI table for the company. The default value is 99 . This field should only be changed in consultation with GSI. Threshold The reconciliation threshold amount, expressed in the company's base currency. Variances whose absolute value falls at or below this amount are treated as within tolerance. Example If the Threshold is set to 100.00 and a variance of $87.50 is calculated for a period, that variance is flagged as within tolerance on the Reconciliation page and does not require a journal entry. A variance of $250.00, by contrast, is flagged as exceeding tolerance and requires investigation. Click Save Changes when all modifications are complete. Company Management Video Tutorial

Adding a Company License Company numbers in RapidReconciler are license-controlled and can only be added by GSI staff. Each company in the application corresponds to a licensed JD Edwards company number as specified in your purchase agreement. Requesting Additional Companies If you have additional companies you would like to reconcile that are not currently available in the application, please contact GSI support to begin the licensing process. Email GSI Sales gsisales@getgsi.com Compose Pre-Filled Email Clicking the button above opens your default email client with a draft addressed to GSI Sales, including a subject line and a request template ready to fill in. The pre-filled email looks like this: Preview — opens in your default mail client To gsisales@getgsi.com Subject RapidReconciler — Additional Company License Request Hello GSI Sales Team, We would like to add one or more additional companies to our RapidReconciler license. Please find the details below. == Customer Information == Company Name: RapidReconciler Account / Customer ID: Primary Contact Name: Primary Contact Email: Phone: == Companies to Add == Please add the following JD Edwards companies to our license: 1. JDE Company Number: Company Name: 2. JDE Company Number: Company Name: (add additional rows as needed) == Additional Notes == Thank you, If you prefer to write the email manually, please include the following: Your company name and RapidReconciler account details The JD Edwards company numbers you would like to add The corresponding company names A GSI representative will follow up to confirm licensing and coordinate the update. Managing Company Settings Company settings, including enabling or disabling companies for individual users, are managed by your RapidReconciler administrator. If you need changes to your company configuration, please contact your internal RapidReconciler administrator. Note Changes to company licensing may require a restart of the RapidReconciler Agent before they take effect in the application.

User Management Add, edit, and remove RapidReconciler users. Configure their authorized functions, visible tabs, and which companies they may access in each module. Navigation User management is accessed by navigating to Admin > Users in the main navigation panel. The Users page displays a list of all current users in the RapidReconciler application, showing their active status, full name, username, and available options. Admin > Users The user grid. The New User button is in the top right; each row has lock, edit, and delete icons in the Options column. User Actions The following actions are available from the Users page: Action How to Access Add a new user Click the New User button in the top right corner. Edit user details Click the paper/pencil icon in the center of the Options column. Edit user rights Click the lock icon on the left of the Options column. Delete a user Click the trash can icon on the right of the Options column, then confirm the prompt. Adding or Editing a User Clicking the New User button or the paper/pencil icon opens the user details pop-up window: New User Dialog The popup that opens when adding or editing a user. The green check next to Password indicates the entered value meets complexity requirements. Complete the following fields: Field Description Full Name The first and last name of the user. Email The company email address of the user. The user will automatically receive an email regarding the confidentiality policy upon account creation. Password Assign an initial password. The minimum length is 8 characters. If complex passwords have been enabled for your company, the password must meet those requirements. A green check appears next to the field label when the entered value meets the configured requirements. Temporary password (1 day) Set to true to require the user to reset their password after 1 day. Recommended for new users. Active Set to true for active users. Setting this to false prevents the user from logging in until it is changed back. Click Save Changes in the lower right when complete to save the user record. Editing User Rights Click the lock icon on the Users page to edit the rights and permissions for a specific user. The user rights window is divided into three sections, each controlling a different layer of access: Authorized Functions — what high-impact operations the user can perform Authorized Tabs — which modules the user can see in the navigation Authorized Companies — which companies the user can view within each visible module Each layer is independent. Granting access at one layer does not automatically grant it at the others — a user who can see the Inventory tab still needs at least one company enabled in the Authorized Companies matrix to actually view inventory data. Authorized Functions Import JDE Restart Service In Transit Exclude PO Receipts Suspend Authorized Tabs Inventory In Transit Admin PO Receipts Authorized Companies Company Inventory In Transit PO Receipts 00010 - Acme, Inc. 00050 - Acme, Ltd. Done User Rights Window An example showing a user with no special functions, access to the Inventory and In Transit tabs only, and full company access for both modules. Click Done when all rights have been configured. Authorized Functions Authorized functions control access to specific high-impact operations that go beyond the standard reconciliation workflow. All four functions are off by default — they must be explicitly granted, and most should be reserved for administrators or power users. Function Description Recommended For Import JDE Grants permission to trigger an ad-hoc data import from JD Edwards outside the regular nightly cycle. Because this can affect data consistency for other users mid-period, it is rarely recommended. Only when GSI advises; not needed for normal operation. Restart Service Grants permission to restart RapidReconciler data services from within the application. Used to recover from issues exporting inventory As-Of data to Excel and to clear memory on the application server. The restart process takes approximately 5 to 10 minutes during which the application is unavailable to all users. RapidReconciler administrators only. In Transit Exclude Grants the ability to exclude order pairs in the In Transit module. Exclusions affect what is reported as in transit and require investigation in JD Edwards before being applied. Administrators and senior reconcilers. PO Receipts Suspend Grants the ability to suspend purchase orders from contributing to the PO Receipts reconciliation. Has similar implications to In Transit Exclude. Administrators and senior reconcilers. Principle of least privilege Grant Authorized Functions only to users who need them for their job. The Restart Service permission in particular affects every other active user, so it should be tightly held. Authorized Tabs Authorized tabs control which modules are visible in the main navigation panel. This is the coarsest layer of access — a tab that is unchecked here will not appear in the user's navigation at all, regardless of any company-level access configured below. Tab What It Reveals Inventory The Inventory module — perpetual vs. GL reconciliation, transactions, As-Of, and integrity reports. In Transit The In Transit module — transfer order reconciliation, orders, transactions, and integrity reports. PO Receipts The PO Receipts module — Received-Not-Vouchered reconciliation. Admin The administration module — companies, users, general settings, and offset accounts. Only grant this to RapidReconciler administrators. Common configurations Inventory analyst: Inventory tab only. In Transit specialist: Inventory and In Transit tabs. Full reconciler: Inventory, In Transit, and PO Receipts tabs. Administrator: All four tabs, including Admin. Authorized Companies The Authorized Companies section is a matrix that controls which companies the user can view, broken down by module. Each row is a licensed company; each column is a module the user has access to (per the Authorized Tabs section above). This per-module breakdown means access can be tailored precisely. A reconciler responsible for Inventory across all U.S. entities but only In Transit at headquarters can be configured exactly that way — Inventory checked for every U.S. company, In Transit checked only for the headquarters company. Behavior Only companies the user has access to appear in the company filter dropdowns within the application. Reconciliation totals shown to the user only include authorized companies — figures and variances on the Reconciliation page reflect only what the user is permitted to see. If a module column is unchecked for every company, the user will see the module's tab (if granted in Authorized Tabs) but no data within it. If Authorized Tabs does not include a particular module, that module's column in this matrix has no effect — the user cannot reach the module to use the access. Adding new companies When a new company is added to your RapidReconciler license, it appears in this matrix unchecked for every existing user. Each user who needs access to the new company must be updated individually. Click Done when all rights have been configured. Common Pitfalls User can't see a company they should have access to Both layers must be granted: (1) the relevant module must be checked under Authorized Tabs , and (2) the company must be checked in the matching column under Authorized Companies . If either is missing, the user won't see the data. User sees the tab but no data inside it The Authorized Tabs section grants visibility of the module, but the per-company matrix controls what data appears inside. Confirm at least one company is checked in that module's column. Reconciliation totals look wrong for a user Reconciliation figures shown to a user only include companies they are authorized for in the relevant module. If a user reports unexpected totals, verify their Authorized Companies matrix matches the scope of their job. Deleting a User To delete a user, click the trash can icon to the right of their name on the Users page and confirm the prompt when displayed. User Management Video Tutorial

Start Here — RR Administrators Configure and maintain RapidReconciler with confidence. This guide is the wayfinding entry point for RapidReconciler administrators — covering company licensing, user permissions, general settings, and inventory offset accounts. If you are new to the role, the topics under Getting Oriented in the sidebar tell you what RapidReconciler asks an administrator to manage and the recommended order to do it in. Each procedural section is also self-contained and can be jumped to directly when you are looking up a specific task. Main Navigation INVENTORY IN TRANSIT PO Receipts ADMIN Companies Users General Inventory Offsets If You Are New to the Role Read the three Getting Oriented topics in order before working through the procedures. They cover the shape of the job and the recommended sequence for an initial deployment, so the procedural topics make sense as steps in a flow rather than a list of features. Read in this order What it covers What You Configure The four configuration domains an administrator owns and how they relate to each other. Your First Setup The recommended order to configure things in for a first deployment, with links into the procedural topics. Pre-launch Checklist What must be configured and verified before users can productively work in the application. Quick Reference — Common Tasks I need to add a new fiscal year start date Company Management A new hire needs login access User Management I need to license an additional JDE company Adding a Company License The Roll Forward report is missing a column General Variance entries are populating with the wrong accounts Inventory Offsets A user can’t see a company they should have access to User Management Procedural Topics The full procedural reference. Self-contained — each topic includes step-by-step navigation, field references, and links to related video tutorials where available. Section Description About Administration Orientation for the administrator role and the four areas this manual covers. Company Management Configure licensed companies, fiscal start dates, currencies, AAI documents, and thresholds. Request a License How to request an additional company or user license from GSI Sales, and what to include in the email. User Management Add, edit, and remove users. Set authorized functions, tabs, and per-module company access. General Common units of measure, roll forward report configuration, and cardex deletion. Inventory Offsets Configure GL accounts for End of Day and Transactional variance journal entries. Resetting the Database When (rarely) a full RR database reset is appropriate, what it impacts, and how to start the conversation with RR Support. Reference Use these on demand. Self-Check is a useful read-through after the Getting Oriented topics; Vocabulary is a lookup utility. Section Description Self-Check Six scenario questions to test whether the doc landed. Not graded. Vocabulary RapidReconciler-specific terms relevant to the administrator role. Looking up a specific task? Jump straight to the relevant procedural topic from the sidebar, or use the Quick Reference above. The Getting Oriented topics are most useful on initial deployment; experienced admins can skip them.

What You Configure Five minutes of orientation. RapidReconciler asks an administrator to manage four configuration domains, all reached from the Admin menu in the main navigation. Knowing what they are — and how they relate — makes the procedural topics much easier to navigate. Where They Live in the Application From any page, the four configuration areas are reached by clicking Admin in the left-rail main navigation, which expands to show the four sub-items below: Main Navigation INVENTORY IN TRANSIT PO Receipts ADMIN Companies Users General Inventory Offsets Admin menu, expanded Companies, Users, General, and Inventory Offsets are the four configuration screens you’ll work with. The Four Domains Domain What It Covers Procedural Topic Companies The licensed JDE companies RapidReconciler can see. Fiscal year start dates, base currency, AAI document types, and tolerance thresholds for each licensed company. Company Management + Adding a Company License Users Who can log in. What each user can see (which companies, which modules) and do (which functions, which tabs). The boundary between an inventory accountant and an A/P clerk is drawn here. User Management General Application-wide configuration that doesn’t belong to a specific company or user. Common units of measure for cross-item analysis, the Roll Forward report column mapping, and cardex purge controls. General Inventory Offsets The GL accounts RapidReconciler suggests as offsets when end-users post variance journal entries. Configure these correctly and end-users get clean Excel exports they can paste into JDE without editing. Inventory Offsets How They Relate The four domains are mostly independent, but a few dependencies are worth knowing up front: Companies must exist before users can be assigned to them. A user’s per-module company access list (Inventory, In Transit, PO Receipts) is filtered to companies that are licensed and configured. Configure companies first. Offset accounts depend on companies and on the AAI document types each company uses. If a company changes its AAI document type configuration, the offset account mappings should be reviewed. General settings — particularly the Common UOM list — affect what end-users see on the As-Of page. Changes here are visible to all users immediately on the next page load. What You Do Not Configure Worth being explicit. RapidReconciler is read-only against JD Edwards. The configuration items below live in JDE, not in RapidReconciler — an administrator should know they exist but they aren’t managed here: DMAAI table 4152. Inventory accounts come from DMAAI; managed in JDE. GL chart of accounts. Owned by the JDE finance configuration. JDE security or row-security. RapidReconciler trusts what JDE has authorized; it does not enforce its own row-level security on JDE data. The nightly import schedule. Operationally owned by the team that runs the JDE-to-RapidReconciler import jobs. See About Administration for a fuller orientation, including how the administrator role is assigned. Try this now If you have admin access to RR, log in and click through the four sub-items under the Admin menu in order: Companies → Users → General → Inventory Offsets . Don’t change anything — just look at each page and notice what’s configurable on it. The goal is recognition, not understanding. Five minutes here will make every procedural topic in this walkthrough easier to follow because you’ll have already seen the screen each topic is describing.

Your First Setup The recommended order to configure RapidReconciler for a first deployment. Each step links into the procedural topic that explains how to do it. Following the order matters — some steps depend on earlier ones. Recommended Sequence # Action Why This Order 1 Read About Administration . Confirms how the administrator role is assigned and gives you the vocabulary for the rest of the setup. 2 Configure each licensed company. See Company Management . Fiscal year start, base currency, AAI document types, tolerance thresholds. Everything downstream filters by company, so getting this right first prevents rework later. 3 Configure General. See General . Common UOM list and Roll Forward report column mapping. These are application-wide and worth configuring once before users start running into them. 4 Configure inventory offsets. See Inventory Offsets . Required for clean variance JE exports. Without this, end-users will see “TBD” placeholders in their Excel exports. 5 Add users and assign access. See User Management . Users are added last because per-module company access depends on companies being configured first. Adding users earlier means revisiting them after company setup. 6 Walk the Pre-launch Checklist before announcing availability to end-users. Confirms each domain is set up correctly and that a representative end-user can productively work in the application before broader rollout. If You Are Adding a Company After Initial Deployment The sequence above describes a first deployment. Once RapidReconciler is live and you need to add a newly-licensed JDE company: Submit the company licensing request to GSI support — see Adding a Company License . Once GSI confirms the license is in place, configure the new company per Company Management . Review and update offset account mappings if the new company introduces new AAI document types. Update existing user permissions to grant per-module access to the new company where appropriate. If You Are Inheriting an Existing Deployment If you are taking over administration of a RapidReconciler instance that someone else configured: Walk the Pre-launch Checklist as a sanity check — treat it as a configuration audit rather than a setup procedure. Pay particular attention to user access lists. Stale users (departed staff still configured) are the most common finding when an administrator changes hands. Verify offset account mappings against the chart of accounts. Account numbers do change, and stale offsets produce wrong entries on user JE exports. Review the Common UOM list for entries that no longer reflect current item-master conventions. When you’re done with the sequence, walk the Pre-launch Checklist to confirm the deployment is ready for end-users. Try this now For new deployments: identify which step from the recommended sequence you’re currently on and what’s blocking the next one. The most common blockers are waiting for company licensing from GSI or waiting for end-user lists from the customer’s HR or operations team. Knowing where you’re stuck is the first step to unstuck. For inherited deployments: walk the Pre-launch Checklist now — not as a setup procedure but as a configuration audit. Most inherited deployments have at least one finding (usually stale users or stale offset mappings). Catching them on a quiet day is far better than discovering them at period close.

Pre-launch Checklist What must be configured and verified before users can productively work in the application. Use this for first deployment, as a configuration audit when inheriting an instance, and as a periodic review at major milestones (new fiscal year, new licensed company, organizational changes). Companies Each licensed company is configured The fiscal year start date matches the company’s fiscal calendar in JDE. The base currency is correct. AAI document types are populated and match the document types actually in use in JDE. Tolerance thresholds are set to your organization’s materiality standard (or to the GSI default if you have not yet established one). The company appears in the period selector with reasonable historical periods available. General Application-wide configuration is set The Common UOM list includes the units of measure that span multiple item master records. The Roll Forward report column mapping reflects the document and order types your organization actually uses. The cardex deletion policy matches your organization’s data retention requirements. Inventory Offsets Offset account mappings are complete Every variance source that produces a JE export has an offset account configured. The configured GL accounts exist in the chart of accounts and are open for posting. A test export from the Inventory Offsets widget produces an Excel file with no “TBD” entries other than expected ones. If multiple companies use different offset account schemes, each company’s mappings have been reviewed individually. Users User access is appropriate Every user who needs access has an account. Departed staff have been removed. Each user’s authorized functions and tabs match their role (inventory accountants don’t see PO Receipts admin functions; A/P clerks don’t see inventory configuration). Per-module company access is set correctly for each user. At least one administrator other than yourself exists, so the system isn’t single-administrator-dependent. Operational Readiness The application is ready for end-users The nightly JDE-to-RapidReconciler import has run successfully at least once. Both status lights on the Reconciliation page are green for the periods end-users will work with. A representative end-user from each role family (inventory, A/P) has logged in, completed their Start Here or Start Here orientation, and confirmed they can see what they need to see. End-users know how to contact you (or the support inbox) when something doesn’t look right. What to Do When the Checklist Surfaces a Problem If a company is missing or misconfigured Resolve through Company Management (for already-licensed companies) or Adding a Company License (if the company is not yet licensed). If offset accounts are missing or stale Update the affected mappings in Inventory Offsets . Re-export from the Inventory Offsets widget to verify the change took effect. If user access is wrong Edit the affected user(s) in User Management . For departed staff, deactivate or remove rather than just clearing module access — this keeps the active user list clean. If the nightly import has not run or produced errors This is operational rather than configuration. Contact the team that runs the import jobs (typically IT or the JDE administration team). RapidReconciler administrators do not own the import schedule itself. Try this now The single highest-value pre-launch action is to log in as a representative end-user (or have one of your end-users log in on a call with you) and verify that they can see what they should see. Specifically: do their account filters show the companies and business units they need? Do they see the modules they need (Inventory, In Transit, PO Receipts) and not the ones they don’t? If they look at a closed period, do the GL Balance values match what JDE shows for the same accounts? Most pre-launch surprises surface in the first five minutes of a real end-user logging in. Better to find them now than at period close.

About Administration Orientation for the administrator role within RapidReconciler — including how the role is assigned and the four areas this manual covers. Overview Any RapidReconciler user can be designated as an administrator by enabling the Admin checkbox within their user rights settings. When RapidReconciler is first installed, a GSI Admin account is configured as the initial administrator in order to set up the application. GSI will also designate the first customer user as an administrator. That customer administrator then assumes responsibility for maintaining the RapidReconciler configuration going forward. Areas of Administrator Responsibility This training manual covers the following four areas of RapidReconciler administration. They are accessed from the Admin menu in the main navigation panel — visible only to users who have administrator rights enabled on their account. Admin menu, expanded Click ADMIN in the main navigation to reveal the four administration areas covered in this walkthrough. Area What it covers Company Management Configuring licensed companies and start dates. User Management Adding and modifying RapidReconciler users and permissions. General Roll forward setup, common units of measure, and cardex deletions. Inventory Offsets Setting up offset accounts for End of Day and Transactional variances. Where to begin If you are new to the role, work through the sections in order. If you are looking up a specific task, jump directly to the relevant section from the sidebar.

General Configure common units of measure, define roll forward report columns, and access the cardex deletion utility from the General page. Navigation General settings are accessed by navigating to Admin > General in the main navigation panel. From this page, administrators can configure or execute the following: Common Units of Measure Roll Forward Report Settings Delete RapidReconciler Cardex Records Common Units of Measure Common units of measure allow quantities to be restated in a single unit of measure across all items. This is useful in scenarios where multiple items in a warehouse have different primary units of measure but each has a conversion factor set up for a common unit. Example If hundreds of items in a particular warehouse need to be totaled by weight, and each item has a conversion factor defined for LB (pounds), adding LB as a common unit of measure allows the total weight to be reported across all items in that single unit. Procedure Type the 2-character UOM code into the input field. Click the Add UOM button. The added UOM will be available for use after the next data refresh cycle. Common Units of Measure Video Tutorial Roll Forward Settings The roll forward settings define the columns displayed between the opening and closing balance columns on the Roll Forward page within the Inventory module. Each row in the settings represents one column in the report. Units or amounts are summarized from the item ledger table F4111 based on either the order type (DCTO) or document type (DCT) field. Use the New Setting button in the top right corner to add a new row. The maximum limit is 12 rows or "totals columns." Each setting contains the following fields: Field Description Column Title A user-defined title describing the type of transactions being summarized in that column. Sort The sequence of the column in the report output. The order can be manually adjusted. Field Determines whether the total is calculated using the order type (DCTO) or document type (DCT). Codes 1 through 6 The specific order types or document types to be summarized in that column. The maximum is 6 codes per row. If additional codes are required, add a new setting. To delete a setting, click the trash can icon at the far right of the row. To edit an existing setting, click the paper/pencil icon . When editing, key the desired values into the applicable fields. For fields with drop-down arrows, select the value from the list provided. Click Save Changes when complete. Sample Roll Forward Configuration Title Sort Field 1 2 3 4 5 6 Receipts 1 DCTO OP OL OO OT NetWO 2 DCTO WO W1 WR Adjustments 3 DCT PI IA II IR IT IB Transfers 4 DCTO ST S2 S3 S5 S6 S7 Shipments 5 DCTO SO SI SJ SM S8 S9 Returns 6 DCTO CO C2 CW WriteDowns 7 DCT WD Resulting Report Layout With the configuration above, the Roll Forward report renders the columns shown below. Each totals column appears between the Opening Balance and Closing Balance in the order set by the Sort field: ROLL FORWARD — INVENTORY Period: 12/2025 · Company: 00010 Acme, Inc. Item OPENING BALANCE Receipts NetWO Adjustments Transfers Shipments Returns WriteDowns CLOSING BALANCE Item-A100 12,450 3,200 (150) 75 (420) (1,800) 120 0 13,475 Item-B205 8,300 1,600 0 25 (150) (940) 60 (50) 8,845 Item-C310 5,750 2,100 (80) 0 (220) (1,100) 45 0 6,495 Total 26,500 6,900 (230) 100 (790) (3,840) 225 (50) 28,815 Sample Roll Forward Output The seven totals columns from the configuration above appear between Opening and Closing Balance, in Sort order. Each value summarizes the matching DCTO or DCT codes from JDE for the period. Roll Forward Settings Video Tutorial Delete RapidReconciler Cardex Records This function allows administrators to delete cardex records from the RapidReconciler database. A training video is available within the application to guide administrators through this process. Caution This is an administrative function that should be used with caution. Refer to the in-application video for full guidance before proceeding. Delete Cardex Records Video Tutorial

Request a License When the customer needs to add a new JDE company to RapidReconciler, more user seats beyond the current count, or any other expansion to their existing RR entitlement, the request goes to GSI Sales . License changes are not handled by RR Support — Sales owns the entitlement, RR Support owns the running application. What to Include in Your Email Send a short email to gsisales@getgsi.com with the items below. Sales will reply to confirm the change is in place and to coordinate any billing follow-up. Customer / instance name — so Sales can match the request to your account. What you need to add — e.g., “license for JDE company 00050 ” or “five additional user seats”. When you need it by — especially if there’s a go-live, audit, or period-close deadline. Who to copy on the response — procurement, project lead, IT, the user’s manager. Quick mailto Email gsisales@getgsi.com with the four items above. After Sales Confirms Once Sales replies that the license is in place: For a new company license , follow Your First Setup → If You Are Adding a Company After Initial Deployment to configure the new company in RapidReconciler. For additional user seats , follow User Management to add the users and grant module / company access.

Resetting the RapidReconciler Database A database reset wipes RR’s stored snapshots of JDE data and the corrections an administrator has layered on top. It is a heavyweight, irreversible operation. RR Support performs the reset — administrators request it, but do not run it themselves. Before requesting Schedule a discovery call with RR Support to discuss whether a reset is actually the right answer. The vast majority of “the numbers look wrong” situations are not solved by a reset — they’re solved by a configuration change, posting a missing batch, or a roll-forward correction. A reset should be the last option considered, not the first. When a Reset May Be Recommended Resets are rarely the right tool. The situations where RR Support has historically agreed a reset was appropriate include: Significant chart-of-accounts restructuring in JDE that invalidates RR’s historical inventory account mappings. GL class code overhaul — widespread renumbering or remapping of GL classes that breaks the reconciliation history’s account assignments. Cardex baseline change — a JDE-side cardex rebuild or recosting that makes RR’s prior cardex snapshots irrelevant. Even in these situations, RR Support will work through the impact with you before scheduling the reset. The decision is collaborative. Important Scope A reset is instance-wide . It affects every company configured in RapidReconciler — companies cannot be reset in isolation. If only one company has issues, a reset is almost certainly not the right path; work with RR Support on a company-scoped correction instead. What Happens After a Reset After RR Support completes the reset, RapidReconciler rebuilds its snapshots from JDE on the next scheduled job. Expect: Reconciliation history (period-by-period RR snapshots) populates forward from the reset date, not backfilled retroactively. Administrator-applied corrections (inventory offsets, suspension and exclusion rules) need to be re-evaluated — some carry forward, some don’t, depending on what the reset cleared. RR Support will confirm specifics for your case. End-users may see “no data” for prior periods until the next nightly job completes. How to start the conversation Email rrsupport@getgsi.com with a description of the symptoms you’re seeing and what changed in JDE. Ask for a 30-minute call to discuss whether a reset is appropriate. Do not request the reset directly — the call is the gate.

Self-Check Six scenarios to test your reading. Read each one, decide what you’d do, and click Where to find this to confirm. If a scenario surprises you or you’d guess wrong, that’s the topic to re-read — not a failure. Administrator decisions tend to be infrequent and high-consequence; the value of these scenarios is in surfacing the judgment patterns rather than memorizing facts. Scenario 1 A new RapidReconciler deployment. You’ve been given admin access. The customer has three licensed JDE companies and you need to onboard ten end-users (six inventory accountants, four AP clerks). What order do you configure things in — companies first, users first, settings first? Why? Where to find this: Your First Setup → Recommended Sequence Scenario 2 You’re inheriting an existing RapidReconciler instance from a previous admin. The instance has been running for two years. What’s the most common configuration finding in a handoff like this, and what tool does this doc give you to surface it? Where to find this: Your First Setup → If You Are Inheriting an Existing Deployment Scenario 3 An end-user reports that their Offset Account widget Excel exports are showing “TBD” in the offset column for several variance rows. What’s the configuration gap, and which procedural topic do you fix it in? Where to find this: Pre-launch Checklist → Inventory Offsets Scenario 4 An end-user opens a support ticket: “The Reconciliation page shows totally wrong numbers for company 00010 today — way off from what JDE shows.” Is this likely a configuration issue you own, an operational issue someone else owns, or a user error? What do you check first to decide? Where to find this: Pre-launch Checklist → What to do when the checklist surfaces a problem Scenario 5 The customer just licensed a new JDE company through GSI. You see the license is in place. End-users are asking when they can start using it. What four things do you do (in order) before announcing the new company is available? Where to find this: Your First Setup → If You Are Adding a Company After Initial Deployment Scenario 6 A user complains that they can see the Inventory module but not the In Transit module — they need both for their role. Where in the configuration is this controlled, and what specific setting do you change? Where to find this: What You Configure → Users How to use this The questions don’t have a score. They’re a self-test for whether the doc landed. If three or more felt uncertain, the doc didn’t land and is worth a re-read — ideally with the specific topic each scenario points to. If they all felt obvious, you’re ready to walk the Pre-launch Checklist for your specific deployment.

Vocabulary RapidReconciler-specific terms relevant to the administrator role. Each entry is a one-line definition plus a link to the topic that explains it in depth. Skim once for awareness; come back when a term comes up in a configuration screen and you want to remember what it meant. AAI document type Per-company configuration that tells RapidReconciler which JDE document types apply to inventory transactions for that company. Drives the AAI-based account derivation in the Variance Calculation. Company Management . Authorized functions Per-user permissions for what actions a user can take inside a module (e.g., apply a suspension, post a JE export, edit a note). Configured in User Management alongside authorized tabs. Authorized tabs Per-user permissions for which views (tabs) a user sees inside a module. Used to hide irrelevant pages from users whose role doesn’t need them — e.g., an A/P clerk doesn’t need to see the Inventory Reconciliation page. Common UOM Application-wide list of units of measure that span multiple item master records, used to restate quantities across items for analysis on the As-Of page. Configured in General . Company license The mechanism that grants RapidReconciler access to a JDE company’s data. Licensed at the GSI level; configured at the customer level. Adding a previously-unlicensed company requires a request to GSI support. Adding a Company License . Fiscal year start date Per-company configuration that tells RapidReconciler when each company’s fiscal year begins. Drives period boundaries throughout the application. Must match what JDE has for the same company. Offset account The GL account configured to be used as the offset side of a variance journal entry. Configured per company per variance source. When end-users export from the Inventory Offsets widget, the configured offset is filled in automatically; missing offsets show as “TBD”. Inventory Offsets . Per-module company access The list of companies a user can see inside a specific module (Inventory, In Transit, PO Receipts). A user can have access to different sets of companies in different modules. Configured per-user in User Management . Roll Forward report column mapping Configuration that maps JDE document and order types to columns on the Roll Forward report. If end-users see a non-zero value in the Variance column at the right, a document or order type is missing from the mapping. General . Suggested Entry popup The end-user-facing popup that uses configured offset accounts to pre-build a variance JE for the user to review and post. Quality of this popup is directly proportional to the quality of offset account configuration. Tolerance threshold Per-company materiality threshold used to filter the Transactions page so end-users see only meaningful variances rather than sub-unit rounding noise. Differences below the threshold are excluded by default but still roll up into the Transactions line of the headline variance. Tolerance Adjust line An informational line on the Offset Account export representing the aggregate of sub-tolerance differences. Shown to end-users so they know it exists; they replace it with the correct GL account before pasting into JDE. Back to Index for the full topic table.

Comparing RR to JDE reports Your RapidReconciler totals don’t match the report you’re used to running in JD Edwards. Most of the time the difference is timing (RR and the JDE report were taken at different moments) or method (the two reports are computing slightly different things). Walk the symptom that matches what you’re seeing. Pick your symptom If you’re seeing… Go to The JDE Stock Valuation Report total doesn’t match the RR Inventory As-of total for the same period Stock Valuation total differs RR shows a calculated cost for an item that doesn’t match the cost on the JDE item record Calculated cost differs from JDE A transaction appears in a different period in RR than the period its JDE GL date would suggest Cardex date logic The sum of cardex transactions for an item doesn’t equal the on-hand JDE shows — quantity-level drift , not a date-placement issue Cardex variance RR’s DMAAI Entry Integrity report (Integrity Report 2) and the JDE DMAAI’s analyzer disagree on how many AAI issues exist DMAAI scope difference None of the above, or the steps in a topic above didn’t resolve it Email RR Support Quick Reference Why the totals differ The JDE Stock Valuation Report is a snapshot — the values as of the moment you ran it. RR’s As-of view summarizes cardex transactions through the period you select. Different definitions, often slightly different totals. First check Make sure both reports are pinned to the same as-of date and the same set of branches / business units. Most discrepancies vanish once the inputs match. Calculated cost RR’s calculated cost is amount on hand ÷ quantity on hand — a derived value, not pulled from JDE’s cost record. JDE has no cost history, so RR shows the calculated value as a reference. Period assignment RR places each cardex transaction into a fiscal period using a deterministic rule based on the JDE GL date and creation date. See Cardex date logic for the full rule and how Inventory Basis vs. GL Basis accounting changes which period a transaction lands in. Cardex variance When the sum of an item’s F4111 cardex rows doesn’t equal its current F41021 on-hand, the difference is cardex variance . This is a different problem than the date logic — it’s a quantity-level drift between the two JDE tables themselves. See Cardex variance for typical causes and how to identify it. Support email rrsupport@getgsi.com

section_title: Stock Valuation Report total differs from RR
body: Stock Valuation Report total differs from RR You ran the JDE Stock Valuation Report for a period and the total doesn’t match what RapidReconciler shows on the Inventory As-of view for the same period. Almost always one of two things: the two reports were taken at different moments ( timing ), or they’re computing slightly different quantities ( method ). Why the totals can legitimately differ The two reports answer slightly different questions: JDE Stock Valuation Report — a snapshot of inventory value at the moment you ran the report. It pulls the current quantity-on-hand from the JDE item branch tables and applies the current item cost. RR Inventory As-of view — a reconstruction of inventory value as of a date you specify, built from the cardex transactions (F4111) and roll-forward / roll-back logic. It’s designed to give you a consistent “point in time” figure that survives subsequent activity. If the JDE report is taken hours, days, or weeks apart from the RR view you’re comparing it to, the totals won’t match. That’s expected, not a bug. How rounding causes a residual amount Even with the same data and the same as-of moment, the two reports can disagree by a few cents per line because of where the rounding happens in the math. In JDE, item quantities can carry 0 to 6 decimal places and unit costs are typically stored to 4 decimals . When the Stock Valuation Report multiplies these together, the extended value is rounded to 2 decimals for display. The Stock Valuation Report uses one fresh quantity × unit cost calculation per line at report time. RapidReconciler, by contrast, summarizes the cardex transaction amounts the way they were posted — each transaction was already rounded to 2 decimals when JDE wrote it, so the running total is a sum of pre-rounded values, not a single multiplication done at report time. Worked example An item with quantity = 3.142857 on hand and unit cost = 12.3456 : JDE Stock Valuation: 3.142857 × 12.3456 = 38.79989952 , rounded to display as $38.80 . RR Inventory As-of: if the on-hand of 3.142857 was built from three separate transactions (each rounded to 2 decimals when posted), the summed amount might land at $38.81 — a $0.01 residual from the same underlying movement. Across thousands of items the residuals accumulate to a small but real difference in the totals. The most visible expression of this is a line on the As-of view that shows zero quantity but a non-zero residual amount — the cardex transactions for that item zeroed out the quantity perfectly but left a few cents of cumulative rounding behind. Why we don’t hide these lines Some customers ask us to suppress the zero-quantity-with-residual lines from the As-of details view. We don’t, because removing them creates a mismatch between the total on the Inventory Reconciliation page and the total on the As-of details page for the same period. Other customers explicitly want these residuals visible because they investigate them as part of their close. Both views always tie out by design. How JDE handles these residuals (and why some still show up) JDE has its own mechanism for clearing residual value at zero quantity: Zero Balance Adjustments (ZBAs). When a triggering transaction drives on-hand to zero and leaves residual cost behind, JDE auto-generates a journal entry that writes off the residual to a designated account (configured via DMAAI 4126 / 4128 for Inventory, 4400 / 4405 for Purchasing). The cardex row for the ZBA shows “Zero Balance Adjustment” in the Explanation field. ZBAs only fire on specific transaction types, though: Triggers a ZBA: inventory issue (P4112), inventory transfer (P4113), negative inventory adjustment (P4114), negative-quantity PO receipt or receipt reversal (P4312). Does NOT trigger a ZBA: sales shipments (P4205 / R42800), inventory reclassification (P4116), cycle / tag count updates (R41413 / R41610), future cost updates (R41052), cost revisions (P4105). That last bullet is the gap. The residuals you typically see on RR’s As-of view are the ones whose final zero-out transaction was a sales shipment, a cycle count, a cost revision, or any other non-ZBA path — JDE’s own write-off mechanism never fires for those, so the residual stays in the cardex history and surfaces in RR. Step 1 — Confirm the as-of dates match Note the exact as-of date and time of the JDE Stock Valuation Report. If the report doesn’t print one, the as-of is “the moment it ran.” In RR, set the Inventory As-of view to the same as-of date . Use the period selector in the page header to pick the matching month / period. Re-run both. If the totals now match (or are very close), the original gap was just timing. Tip Same date isn’t always the same moment . If the JDE report ran at 9 AM and a large goods receipt posted at 10 AM, RR’s end-of-day As-of for that date will already include the receipt that the JDE 9 AM report doesn’t. Run both as close together as you can, ideally end-of-day after activity has settled. Step 2 — Confirm both reports cover the same scope If the totals are still off after aligning the dates, check that both reports are filtered to the same set of records: Branch / plant — if JDE is filtered to one branch and RR is showing all branches, the totals won’t match. Company / business unit — same idea. Confirm both reports are scoped to the same companies. Item types — some Stock Valuation Report variants include or exclude certain item types (e.g., outside operations, kit components). Check the version specs on the JDE side. Step 3 — If the dates and scope match, look at the variance sources Once the inputs are aligned and the figures still differ, the gap is meaningful and worth investigating. Open the Inventory Reconciliation page in RR and check the variance source breakdown : Cardex variance — the sum of F4111 cardex transactions doesn’t match the F41021 on-hand. Often a deleted cardex row or a direct F41021 adjustment without a matching F4111 entry. Whole-unit dollar gaps, not a few-cents residual. Period placement — the date logic put a transaction in a different period than JDE’s GL date implies. Show up as case-level quantity gaps at period boundaries. End-of-day — sales orders or work orders that haven’t settled yet. Open the Inventory Reconciliation page and drill into the non-zero variance category to see which items contribute. If a single line is causing the gap, RR’s detail view will show it directly. If you’re running a custom or self-generated report The guidance above assumes you’re comparing RR to the standard JDE Stock Valuation Report . If your team is comparing RR to a custom report — a Smart Form, a UBE you wrote in-house, an Excel export with bespoke logic — we can’t comment on the gap without knowing the exact report definition: which tables it pulls from, how it filters, whether it rounds the same way, and where the as-of cut-off sits. The first step in those cases is to document the report’s logic, then match RR’s scope and dates to it the same way you would for the standard report. When to escalate Email rrsupport@getgsi.com if all of the following are true: You’ve confirmed the JDE report and the RR view are pinned to the same as-of date and the same scope (branch / company / business unit). The variance source breakdown doesn’t explain the gap. The gap is material to the period close.

section_title: Calculated cost in RR doesn’t match the JDE item cost
body: Calculated cost in RR doesn’t match the JDE item cost RR shows a calculated cost for an item that’s different from the cost recorded on the JDE item record. This usually isn’t a bug — the two values are computed differently and represent different things. What “calculated cost” means in RR RR’s calculated cost is a derived value : calculated cost = amount on hand ÷ quantity on hand RR computes it because JDE has no cost history — the item record only carries the current cost. RR’s calculated cost gives you a reference figure derived from the actual ledger amounts and quantities at the period you’re viewing, which is usually what you want for reconciliation. Why the JDE item cost can differ Cost method — the JDE item record cost might be standard (07), weighted average (02), or actual (09), each updated on its own cadence. RR’s calculated cost is amount-divided-by-quantity at the snapshot, which doesn’t map cleanly to any single method. Recent revaluation — if the JDE item cost was revalued recently (manual adjustment, weighted-average update, period roll), the post-revaluation cost won’t match the historical amount-on-hand ÷ quantity-on-hand calculation for prior periods. Cost components — the JDE item cost may include components (overhead, freight, etc.) that aren’t in the inventory ledger amount RR is dividing by. What to check Confirm which cost method JDE is using for the item (look at the cost component table or the cost ledger). The Product Costing Guide in RR University covers the variants. Compare the calculated cost not against today’s JDE item cost but against the JDE cost at the same as-of date . If the item was revalued recently, the historical cost matters more than the current one. If the item is part of a manufactured assembly, check whether outside operations or WIP cost components are skewing the on-hand amount RR is dividing by. When the calculated cost is the right number For period-close reconciliation against the GL, the calculated cost (amount ÷ quantity) is usually the right reference because it ties directly to the ledger amounts you’re reconciling. The JDE item record cost is the right reference for forward-looking pricing and standard-cost variance analysis — not for historical reconciliation. When to escalate Email rrsupport@getgsi.com if: The calculated cost looks materially wrong for the item (e.g., it’s negative, zero with non-zero qty on hand, or off by orders of magnitude). You’ve confirmed the cost method and the as-of-date alignment, and the difference still doesn’t make sense.

section_title: Cardex date logic — how RR assigns a transaction to a period
body: Cardex date logic — how RR assigns a transaction to a period Every cardex transaction (F4111 row) ends up in exactly one fiscal period in RapidReconciler. The rule for picking that period is deterministic, but it isn’t always the JDE GL date you might expect — especially for transactions that haven’t posted to the GL yet, or that carry a future-dated GL. This page explains the rule and the two accounting methods that build on top of it. The four-rule period date For each cardex row, RR walks these rules in order and uses the first one that matches: GL Date exists and is in the future. If the cardex row has a GL date but it’s after today, RR uses today as the period date. (Future-dated GL entries are pulled back to the current period so they don’t silently park value in a future period.) GL Date exists. Otherwise, if there’s any GL date on the row, RR uses the GL date . This is the normal case for posted activity. No GL date and Creation Date is in the future. If the row hasn’t posted to the GL yet AND its creation date is after today, RR uses today . (Same rationale — no parking value in the future.) Use the Creation Date. Otherwise, RR uses the creation date as the period date. This is what happens for cardex activity that hasn’t been batched to the GL yet (work-order completions, sales-order shipments waiting on Sales Update, etc.). Why a transaction may land in a different period than its JDE GL date If a transaction’s GL date is in the future (e.g. a backdated reversal was entered with a forward-dated GL), rule 1 fires and RR places it in the current period — not the future period the GL would put it in. The transaction will move once it actually posts and the GL date catches up. Why the rule looks the way it does Each of the three candidate dates has a trade-off: Candidate Pro Con Transaction date (user-entered) Direct user input Accuracy — depends on whoever entered the row Creation date (system-generated) Always present, system-stamped Global “item update” events can shift it GL date Ties RR to GL postings Doesn’t reflect true inventory timing (date the goods actually moved) RR’s four-rule order balances these — prefer the GL date when it’s available and reasonable, fall back to the creation date when the GL date isn’t there yet, and pull future-dated rows back to today so nothing parks ahead of the reporting cut-off. Inventory Basis vs. GL Basis — the accounting method choice The four-rule period date defines where the cardex row first lands . The accounting method defines what happens when its GL posting later arrives in a different period . There are two options: Inventory Basis (default) The transaction stays in the period it was originally placed in (per the four-rule logic). When the GL posts later in a different period, the variance reclassifies from End of Day Variance to a Period Variance . Prior period totals don’t shift around — once a period is closed, its cardex placement is sticky. GL Basis When the GL posts, the cardex transaction moves to the GL’s period . No “Period Variance” classification — everything aligns to GL by construction. Prior periods’ out-of-balance figures can change as late GL postings flow in. Inventory Basis is the default and what most customers use because it preserves period totals once close is complete. GL Basis is occasionally chosen when the customer wants every cardex row to track the GL exactly, even if that means historical totals change retroactively. Confirm which method your instance is on before investigating a period-placement question — the right answer differs between the two. Where this shows up when comparing to JDE A typical mismatch caused by the date logic: A work-order completion happened on the last day of Period 6 but hasn’t been batched to the GL yet. JDE Stock Status, run on Period 7 Day 1, includes the on-hand impact of that completion because the quantity is already in the item-balance tables. RR places the cardex row in Period 6 (rule 4, creation date), so the Period 7 Inventory As-of total in RR excludes it — the totals diverge by exactly that transaction’s extended value. Once the work-order completion is batched and the GL posts, the row reclassifies (Inventory Basis: into a Period Variance staying in Period 6; GL Basis: moves to whatever period the GL date lands in). When you’re reconciling a specific gap and the date logic looks like the cause, drill into the Inventory Reconciliation page’s variance breakdown for the period — End of Day Variance, Period Variance, and Transaction Variance each map to a different scenario in the rules above. How to verify with the As Of Item Details export When the symptom is a quantity-on-hand mismatch between RR and JDE for a specific period — e.g., an item shows 100 LB on the JDE cardex but 80 LB on RR’s As-of view for the same period end — the four-rule period logic is usually the cause. The fastest way to identify which transactions moved is the As Of Item Details export: From the Inventory module, open the Inventory As Of page for the period in question, drill into the item, and export the transaction list to Excel. Each row shows the cardex transaction’s period date as RR assigned it — this is the output of the four-rule logic, not the raw JDE GL date or transaction date. Pull the same item’s F4111 cardex from JDE for the same date range. Compare row by row. The transactions whose RR period date differs from the JDE GL date (or the JDE transaction date, where no GL date exists) are the ones the four-rule logic re-placed. Those are what shifted the As-of quantity. Most of the time the gap comes from one or two transactions whose GL date hadn’t been written when RR processed them — RR fell back to rule 4 (creation date) and placed them in a period the user expected to land elsewhere. Once you can see which transactions moved, you can decide whether to wait for the next nightly job (which will reclassify them once the GL posts) or to investigate further. Date logic vs. cardex variance If the As Of Item Details export shows every RR period date matching the JDE GL / transaction date and the totals still differ, the cause isn’t the date logic — it’s a quantity-level disagreement between F4111 and F41021. See Cardex variance .

section_title: Cardex variance — when F4111 and F41021 disagree
body: Cardex variance — when F4111 and F41021 disagree Cardex variance is the quantity (and dollar) gap between two JDE tables for the same item: F4111 — the Item Ledger, every cardex transaction (receipt, issue, transfer, adjustment, sale, completion). F41021 — the Item Location Balances, the current quantity-on-hand summary by branch / lot / location. For any item, summing all F4111 movements forward from the prior balance forward should equal the F41021 on-hand. When it doesn’t, the difference is cardex variance . This is structurally different from the rounding residuals (which are dollar-level only) and from the date logic (which is a period-placement question, not a quantity question). Why a cardex variance happens F4111 and F41021 are designed to stay in lock-step, but several real-world events break that: Deleted F4111 row. Someone removed a cardex transaction in JDE without an offsetting adjustment. F41021 still reflects the post-transaction on-hand but F4111 no longer has the row that produced it. Direct F41021 adjustment without an F4111 entry. A manual update to the on-hand balance (cycle count fix entered the wrong way, data-fix script, custom UBE) that didn’t write a corresponding cardex row. Failed posting that updated one table but not the other. Most common around abnormally-terminated processes or partially-rolled-back custom workflows. Custom programs that update F41021 directly. Some in-house extensions skip the standard P4112/P4113/P4114 paths and update F41021 in place — if they don’t also write F4111, every such update creates cardex variance. Item Reclassifications (P4116) landing on a different GL account. Not a true variance, but it can look like one in account-level views: reclassifications write paired F4111 entries that move quantity and value between items or between item attributes (GL class, stocking type), and the From and To sides can resolve to different inventory accounts. See Item Reclassifications (P4116) in the cardex reference for the full pattern. How it shows up in JDE-vs-RR comparison The JDE Stock Valuation Report (R5541590) and the Stock Status pull quantity from the F41021 / item-branch tables. RR’s Inventory As-of view rebuilds the on-hand by summing F4111 forward from the balance forward. When F4111 and F41021 disagree, the two reports will too: JDE > RR. F41021 has more on-hand than F4111 would account for — usually a deleted F4111 row, or a direct F41021 fix without a cardex entry. RR > JDE. F4111 has more cumulative quantity than F41021 reflects — usually a missed or rolled-back F41021 update where the cardex row stayed. The dollar gap on this kind of variance is typically a whole-unit-cost multiple (one or more cases times the lot cost), not the few-cents residual you’d see from rounding. How to identify it Open the Inventory Reconciliation page in RR and look at the variance breakdown for the period. If you see a non-zero Cardex variance figure, that’s the symptom — F4111 and F41021 don’t agree for at least one item in the period. Click into the Cardex variance row to see which items contribute. RR lists each item with its F4111-summed quantity vs. its F41021 on-hand. For each contributing item, look at the F4111 history in JDE for unusual gaps (missing transaction numbers, deleted rows) or F41021 adjustments without a matching cardex row. How RR handles it RR doesn’t silently fix cardex variance — that would mask the underlying JDE data issue. Instead, it surfaces the variance on the Reconciliation page so the administrator can investigate and resolve it in JDE (typically by recording the missing F4111 row or backing out the rogue F41021 adjustment). Once F4111 and F41021 agree again, the cardex variance goes to zero on the next nightly refresh. Don’t confuse cardex variance with the date logic Both show up as “the totals don’t match” symptoms. The way to tell them apart: in the As Of Item Details export, if every transaction’s period date matches the JDE GL/transaction date but RR’s summed quantity is still different from JDE’s on-hand, you’re looking at cardex variance, not date logic. The opposite case — period dates don’t match but every cardex row is present on both sides — is date logic.

section_title: DMAAI Entry Integrity vs. JDE DMAAI’s analyzer
body: DMAAI Entry Integrity vs. JDE DMAAI’s analyzer RapidReconciler’s DMAAI Entry Integrity report (Integrity Report 2) and the Export Analyzer’s JDE DMAAI’s template both look at JDE AAIs, but they look at different sets of AAIs . The counts won’t match, and that’s by design. What each one covers RR’s Integrity Report 2 is scoped to the AAIs RapidReconciler actually exercises during reconciliation — primarily the inventory-side AAIs that affect cardex / GL alignment for the items in RR’s reconciliation window. If RR doesn’t exercise an AAI, it won’t appear in the report. This keeps the output focused on what matters for RR-reported variances . The JDE DMAAI’s analyzer reads the full F4095 export from JDE Database Browser (P964001). It sees every Distribution AAI across every JDE module — Inventory (41xx), Sales (42xx), Procurement (43xx), Intercompany (44xx) — whether or not RR ever touches them. It also looks for several anti-patterns that Integrity Report 2 doesn’t check: net-zero clearing pairs, transfer-clearing-account mismatches between 4220 and 4320, duplicate key tuples, and unrecognized AAI series. Why the totals legitimately differ The two reports are answering different questions: Integrity Report 2 — “Are the AAIs RR uses set up correctly for the items in RR’s reconciliation scope?” JDE DMAAI’s analyzer — “Is the customer’s entire F4095 table internally consistent, regardless of whether RR exercises every rule?” A customer will often have far more findings in the JDE DMAAI’s analyzer than in Integrity Report 2 — not because RR is missing problems, but because the analyzer surfaces issues in modules (Sales, Procurement) RR doesn’t reconcile against. Those issues still affect JDE’s GL, just not RR’s reconciliation. How to use them together Start with Integrity Report 2 when reconciling an item-level cardex / GL variance in RR. The findings there map directly to what RR sees. Use the JDE DMAAI’s analyzer when reviewing the broader JDE configuration — especially the transfer-clearing mismatch finding (AAI 4245 ST vs 4320 OT), which often explains hard-to-track Inventory-in-Transit residuals that don’t surface in Integrity Report 2 because they live on the Sales / Procurement sides.
