Disable vs. unschedule Right-click → Disable is the simplest path — it blocks both the schedule and ad-hoc Start Job runs that aren’t yours. The one-shot verification in Step 6 still works because Start Job at Step ignores the disabled state. Alternatively, you can leave the job enabled and just uncheck the schedule (Properties → Schedules tab); use this if there are other operators who may need to run the job manually during the window. 2 JDE Move · Step 2 of 7 Open the SSIS project in Visual Studio You need the project (not just the deployed package). Two paths depending on whether you have the source files: If you have the project files (the .dtproj on the app server or in source control): open it directly via File → Open → Project/Solution . If only the deployed package is available : import from SSISDB. File → New → Project → Integration Services Import Project Wizard , then point at the database server hosting SSISDB and select SSISDB → RapidReconciler → (project name) . The wizard pulls everything back into a fresh project on your local disk that you can edit and re-deploy. 3 JDE Move · Step 3 of 7 Locate the JDE Source connection manager Connection managers live in two places — check the project level first: Project-level : in Solution Explorer (right pane), expand Connection Managers . The JDE Source connection appears here when it’s shared across multiple packages. Package-level : open the .dtsx from Solution Explorer, then look at the Connection Managers tray at the bottom of the design surface. Package-scoped connections live here. The connection manager is typically named JDE Source , JDE , or a similar customer-recognizable label. If you’re not sure which one is the JDE source, right-click each and pick Properties — the ConnectionString property names the JDE host directly. 4 JDE Move · Step 4 of 7 Update and test the connection string Right-click the JDE Source connection manager → Edit . Update the fields with the new connection details: Server / Data Source : new JDE hostname or IP Initial Catalog / Database / Schema : usually unchanged, but confirm Authentication : select the auth method matching the new environment User name / Password : the new service-account credentials For cloud-hosted JDE, click All in the connection dialog and add encryption options if applicable: Encrypt=true , and either TrustServerCertificate=false with the root CA installed on the app server (the secure default), or TrustServerCertificate=true if your security policy allows it (convenient but skips certificate validation — don’t use in production unless you’ve agreed to the trade-off with your security team). Click Test Connection . If it returns Test connection succeeded , you’re good. If it fails, the error message points at the cause — most often a firewall block, an incorrect credential, an untrusted certificate, or a missing / outdated driver. Click OK to save. Don’t embed credentials in cleartext SSIS treats connection-string passwords as a sensitive property. Confirm the project’s ProtectionLevel (Project property page) is EncryptSensitiveWithPassword or EncryptSensitiveWithUserKey — not DontSaveSensitive (which silently drops the password on deploy and the job will fail at runtime). Whichever protection you pick, document the protection password somewhere the next person who edits the project can recover it. 5 JDE Move · Step 5 of 7 Build and redeploy Build → Build Solution ( Ctrl + Shift + B ). Confirm 0 errors in the Output window. Right-click the project in Solution Explorer → Deploy . Step through the deployment wizard. Target the existing SSISDB / RapidReconciler / (project name) path on the database server — you’re overwriting the deployed version with the updated connection. Confirm the wizard ends with Deployment of project complete . See Installing the Production Database → Tour 3 for screenshots of each Visual Studio deployment dialog. 6 JDE Move · Step 6 of 7 Run a one-shot fetch to verify Don’t wait for the next scheduled run — trigger the SQL Agent job manually so you find connection or auth errors immediately. Open SSMS → connect to the RR database server → expand SQL Server Agent → Jobs . Find the RR data-pull job (the one that calls the SSIS package). Right-click → Start Job at Step… → pick the SSIS step → Start . Open Job Activity Monitor to watch progress. A clean run confirms the new connection works end-to-end. Refresh the RapidReconciler UI and confirm fresh data is appearing — the System Status light goes green and the latest run time updates. If the job fails The SSIS error log is the first stop — SSMS → SQL Server Agent → Job Activity Monitor → right-click the failed job → View History , then drill into the failed step and expand the error message. Almost every connection failure is a credential mismatch, a firewall block, a missing root CA (for cloud TLS), or the wrong driver version. The Test Connection in Step 4 catches most of these — if Test Connection passed but the job still fails, the most common cause is that the SQL Agent service account on the database server is a different user from the one you tested with in Visual Studio. Re-test from a command-line tool running as the SQL Agent account to confirm. Don’t skip to Step 7 if Step 6 failed Step 7 re-enables the schedule. If the verification fetch in Step 6 didn’t succeed, leaving the schedule disabled is the correct state — the scheduled job will just fail loudly on the next firing if you re-enable too early. Resolve the failure first (or roll back the connection-string change), then re-run Step 6, then enable. 7 JDE Move · Step 7 of 7 Re-enable the RR data-pull job schedule Once the verification fetch in Step 6 has succeeded and the RR UI is showing fresh data, restore the scheduled run. Open SSMS → SQL Server Agent → Jobs . Right-click the RR data-pull job → Enable . The job icon flips back to the enabled state. (If you used the alternative path in Step 1) Right-click → Properties → Schedules tab → re-check the schedule and click OK . Note the next scheduled run time. Monitor Job Activity Monitor (or just check the RR System Status light) at that time to confirm the first scheduled run completes successfully. If the next scheduled run fails The Step 6 manual fetch ran as your user via Start Job at Step ; the scheduled run uses the SQL Agent service account. A credential mismatch between the two surfaces here. Check the job history (right-click the failed job → View History ) for the auth error, then either grant the SQL Agent service account access to the new JDE database or update the SSIS connection to use a credential the SQL Agent account already has. On-prem to cloud — specific pitfalls When the JDE move is from on-premise to a cloud-hosted environment (Oracle Cloud Database, AWS RDS, IBM Power Virtual Server for DB2 for i, Azure SQL, etc.), the connection-string update itself is the same shape, but several supporting concerns become first-class: Concern What’s different What to do Network latency On-prem JDE typically sits on the same LAN as the RR app server with sub-millisecond round-trips. Cloud JDE adds 10–100 ms per round-trip, often more across regions. Plan for the nightly fetch to run 2–10× longer. Run a test pull during off-hours, time it, and adjust the SQL Agent schedule so the job completes before users arrive. VPN / private link Most cloud JDE deployments don’t expose the database publicly — access is through a site-to-site VPN, an AWS PrivateLink, an Azure Private Endpoint, or an Oracle Cloud FastConnect. Confirm your network team has the tunnel up and the RR app server is on the right route. tnsping (Oracle), db2 connect (DB2), or Test-NetConnection (PowerShell) from the app server is the fastest way to confirm reachability before touching SSIS. Firewall & DNS Outbound rules from the RR app server now have a different destination IP or hostname. Internal DNS may need a new record. Get the new IP from your JDE team and confirm with your network team that outbound on the JDE port is allowed. If using a private DNS zone, confirm the RR app server is configured to resolve it. TLS / SSL Cloud DBs almost always require encrypted connections, and many use a private CA the RR app server doesn’t trust by default. Install the issuing CA into the Windows Trusted Root Certification Authorities store on the RR app server before testing the connection. Use certmgr.msc or PowerShell Import-Certificate . Don’t default to TrustServerCertificate=true — it works but disables certificate validation entirely. Authentication On-prem typically uses Windows-integrated, SQL-auth, or DB2 user / password. Cloud often adds OAuth, Azure AD, federated SSO, or wallet-based connections (Oracle Cloud Autonomous Database). Use the auth method your cloud DBA recommends. For Oracle Cloud Autonomous, that’s usually a TNS wallet — download it from OCI, copy to the app server, set TNS_ADMIN to its location, and use a TNS Service Name connection (not Host / Port). For Azure SQL Managed Instance behind Azure AD, use the Microsoft.Data.SqlClient provider with Authentication=Active Directory Service Principal . Driver version Older drivers (legacy iSeries Access, Oracle 11g client) may not negotiate TLS 1.2 / 1.3 properly or may not support the cloud’s connection topology. Plan to upgrade the driver on the app server as part of the JDE move. For Oracle, install the matching cloud-side client (19c or 21c). For IBM i, install the latest IBM i Access Client Solutions. For SQL Server / Azure SQL, replace Microsoft OLE DB Provider for SQL Server with Microsoft OLE DB Driver 18+ for SQL Server (MSOLEDBSQL18) . Time zones Cloud DBs often run in UTC regardless of where you are; on-prem usually runs in the local time zone. The RR data fetch uses timestamp comparisons that can misbehave when the source TZ shifts. Spot-check timestamps in the first post-move fetch. Pick a specific document, look at the JDE side and the RR side, and confirm the timestamps match. If they’re off by exactly your TZ offset, you have a TZ issue and the SSIS data flow needs explicit conversion in the source query. Egress cost Cloud providers charge for data leaving the cloud. RR’s nightly pull can move gigabytes of cardex / ledger data. Estimate per-pull data volume after a few real runs — your cloud cost dashboard will show it. If the cost is higher than expected, options are: filter the SSIS source query more aggressively, schedule the pull less frequently (weekly summaries + daily deltas), or co-locate the RR app server in the same cloud region as the JDE source to avoid egress entirely. Service-account secrets Cleartext passwords in .dtsx packages are poor practice on-prem and usually fail compliance review for cloud connections. Use SSIS project parameters marked Sensitive for the password. The project ProtectionLevel determines how Sensitive parameters are encrypted at rest. For the more secure option, use a credential-store integration (Azure Key Vault for Azure SQL, OCI Vault for Oracle Cloud) and reference the secret from the SSIS package via an environment variable. Test before cutover For an on-prem → cloud move, the cloud JDE is usually stood up before the on-prem one is retired. Use that overlap. Stand up a second SSIS project (or a second connection-manager configuration) pointing at the new cloud JDE, run a few full fetches against it while the on-prem source is still live, and compare row counts and selected dollar totals before flipping production over. The time-zone, driver, and egress issues above are best caught while you still have the on-prem source as a reference. Next That’s the whole procedure for a customer-side JDE move — nothing to send to rrsupport. If you’d like a quick sign-off list, the Post-Migration Checklist has a JDE-only path at the top.

What to send rrsupport Once your server work is done and you have the IP values captured, email rrsupport@getgsi.com with the values below. GSI updates VALC and coordinates the Cloudflare DNS change on their side — you don’t have direct access to those. What to email Pick the row that matches what you migrated: You replaced… Send these values Application server only New app server internal IP New app server public-facing IP Database server only New database server internal IP Both servers (split onto two machines) New app server internal IP New app server public-facing IP New database server internal IP Both servers (consolidated to one machine) New machine internal IP (used for both app server and DB server in VALC) New machine public-facing IP Double-check before sending Confirm each IP is correct before sending. An incorrect IP entered into VALC will prevent the agent from communicating and block the rest of the cutover — rrsupport will need a second email to fix it. Email template Use this as a starting point — adjust to match what you migrated: To: rrsupport@getgsi.com Subject: Server Migration — VALC update needed (CUSTOMER NAME) Hi rrsupport, We’ve completed our server migration and need VALC + Cloudflare updated to point at the new infrastructure. Customer: CUSTOMER NAME What we replaced: [application server / database server / both] New values: App server internal IP: XX.XX.XX.XX App server public-facing IP: XX.XX.XX.XX Database server internal IP: XX.XX.XX.XX Other changes (if any): [e.g., new domain URL, change in dedicated vs separate topology, anything else worth noting] Our SQL Agent job schedule on the new database server is currently disabled — we’ll enable it once you’ve confirmed VALC and Cloudflare are updated and data is appearing in the UI. Thanks, NAME · CUSTOMER IT What rrsupport does next For your awareness — you don’t need to do anything in this section, but it helps to know what’s happening on the GSI side after your email. Updates VALC. Sets the new IPs on the Client Details and Databases pages. Coordinates the Cloudflare DNS update with GSI I/T to point the public-facing domain at the new app server’s public-facing IP. Confirms back to you when both updates are complete. DNS propagation can take some time after the Cloudflare change — the UI may not load with the new data immediately. After rrsupport confirms Once rrsupport says VALC and Cloudflare are updated and DNS has propagated, log in to the RapidReconciler UI to verify data is appearing. Then enable the SQL Agent job schedule on the new database server. Verify the first scheduled run completes successfully. Next Walk through the Post-Migration Checklist to confirm every step has been completed before signing off.

Post-Migration Checklist End-to-end verification list. Walk this top to bottom before signing off the migration. Skip the sub-sections that don’t apply to what you migrated. Anything unchecked is a blocker for go-live. Pre-Migration Before starting Users notified of the maintenance window Final source-server backup taken (verified) and held as the rollback artifact JDE Server Move (skip if your JDE host isn’t changing) JDE Server Move New JDE connection details collected from your JDE team (host, port, library / schema, service account, TLS / SSL requirement) RR app server has outbound reachability to the new JDE host (firewall + DNS confirmed) Required JDE database driver installed / upgraded on the RR app server (for on-prem → cloud, verify driver version supports TLS 1.2+ and the cloud’s auth method) (Cloud only) Issuing CA installed in the Windows Trusted Root Certification Authorities store on the RR app server RR data-pull SQL Agent job disabled before changing the connection string SSIS JDE Source connection manager updated in Visual Studio; Test Connection succeeded Project rebuilt with 0 errors and re-deployed to SSISDB One-shot SQL Agent run ( Start Job at Step ) completed successfully; fresh data visible in the RR UI (Cloud only) First-run row counts and selected dollar totals spot-checked against the source while it is still live RR data-pull SQL Agent job re-enabled after the verification fetch succeeded First post-cutover scheduled run completed successfully Application Server (skip if not migrating the app server) Application Server Software prerequisites installed on new app server (SSMS, Visual Studio with SSIS, JDE drivers) rrsupport set VALC status to “Created” SSIS package copied to new app server RR Agent installed and showed “Installation Complete” SSIS package deployed to SSISDB on the database server Internal and public-facing IPs captured from the agent log file Database Server (skip if not migrating the DB server) Database Server Software prerequisites installed on new DB server (Integration Services, SSISDB, JDE drivers) Source database backup taken (verified) Database restored on new server and record counts verified against source RRUSER login created with SID mapped from source SQL Agent job recreated with the original schedule, schedule not yet enabled Internal IP of new DB server captured (SQL upgrade only) DMA findings reviewed; compatibility level set; collation verified Sent to rrsupport Email handoff Email sent to rrsupport@getgsi.com with the values matching what was migrated rrsupport confirmed VALC was updated rrsupport confirmed Cloudflare DNS update is in place DNS propagation confirmed (UI loads at your users’ sign-in URL) Validation & Sign-Off Validation Logged in to the RapidReconciler UI; data is visible Spot-checked key figures against source JDE data SQL Agent job schedule enabled on the new DB server First scheduled run completed successfully Users notified migration is complete Done? Every box checked? Migration is complete — archive the source-server backup per your retention policy, hold the source server itself for an agreed sign-off period in case you need to roll back, and document any customer-specific notes that emerged during cutover for the next time.

Start Here — A/P Clerks A wayfinding guide for accounts payable clerks new to RapidReconciler. JD Edwards experience is assumed throughout. The focus here is the PO Receipts module — the Received-Not-Vouchered (RNV) reconciliation, suspensions and exclusions, and the AP-side journal entries that clear what voucher matching alone cannot. i Module availability. If you can’t find the PO Receipts module in your main navigation, contact your RapidReconciler administrator. They will either grant you access (if the module is already enabled at your site) or open a request with GSI support to enable it. Who This Is For You are responsible for some part of the AP cycle that interacts with PO Receipts: investigating open RNV balances, deciding when to suspend or exclude an order, posting offsetting journal entries to clear unvouchered residuals, and supporting period-end close on the RNV account. This guide assumes you already understand JDE’s P4314 voucher match process, what F43121 is, and how Match Type 1 (receipts) and Match Type 2 (vouchers) records combine to drive the RNV account. What it does is map that JDE knowledge to RapidReconciler’s vocabulary — particularly the Suspension and Exclusion mechanisms, which exist only in RapidReconciler — and tell you what to read, in what order, in your first month. What “Ready” Looks Like Use these milestones to gauge your own progress. They are not prescriptive timelines — some sites move faster, some take longer — but they are the typical learning arc for a JDE-experienced AP user. Day 1 Logged in. Can find the PO Receipts module, the unreconciled view, and the RNV variance summary. Can identify your accounts in the filter. Week 1 Can drill from the variance summary into a specific order, read the F43121 vs GL match status, and explain when an order should be suspended, excluded, or left alone. Month 1 Has applied at least one suspension and one exclusion with a defensible audit-trail note. Has posted an offsetting JE in JDE that cleared a known-unresolvable RNV balance. Topics Topic Read When What RapidReconciler Does First. Five minutes that map JDE concepts to where you’ll find them in RapidReconciler. Your First Session Day 1. A 10-minute guided first sit-down at the PO Receipts page. Your First Week Week 1. The four documents to read — in order — and which ones to defer. Your First Period-End Close When period-end is on the calendar. The buddy and mock-close recommendations, plus the top three new-user mistakes specific to RNV reconciliation. Self-Check End of week 1. Six scenario questions to test whether the doc landed. Not graded. Vocabulary Anytime. RapidReconciler-specific terms that aren’t standard JDE. i Recommended at most sites. Pair with an experienced reviewer for your first period-end close, and walk through a closed prior period (in production or in a non-production environment) before doing one live. Neither is enforced — both noticeably reduce first-close stress. i Key principle. A suspension or exclusion in RapidReconciler does not change F43121 or the GL. It is an annotation that says “this item is known and accepted, here is why.” If the GL is wrong, fix the GL first — never use these mechanisms to hide a real problem.

What RapidReconciler Does (and Doesn’t) Five minutes of orientation. The aim is to translate what you already know about JDE into where it lives and what it’s called inside RapidReconciler — and to set boundaries on what RapidReconciler can and cannot change. The Read-Only Model RapidReconciler does not write to JDE. It reads from JDE on a nightly import cycle, compares the data, and surfaces discrepancies. Every correction — every voucher match, every JE, every PO cancellation — is posted in JD Edwards using the tools you already know. RapidReconciler then picks up the change at the next nightly refresh. RapidReconciler can change Only JD Edwards can change Notes attached to a PO order or line; suspensions (whole order) and exclusions (single line); filter and view preferences; report exports. F43121 (the PO Receiver table), the GL, every Match Type 1 receipt and Match Type 2 voucher record — everything that matters for the RNV reconciliation. The practical implication: if you spot an unresolvable RNV residual at 2pm and post a clearing JE in JDE at 3pm, the F43121 record will still show as open in RapidReconciler until tomorrow’s import. The suspension or exclusion mechanism (which is in RapidReconciler) is what removes the item from the variance calculation in the meantime. What It’s Comparing The headline question RapidReconciler answers for PO Receipts is one you already know how to ask: does the open balance in F43121 agree to the GL balance for the RNV account? In a clean environment, every Match Type 1 (receipt) record is eventually offset by a Match Type 2 (voucher) record, the F43121 open balance reduces to what is genuinely awaiting voucher, and the GL agrees. In practice, certain records will never clear through the standard process — receipts cleared by manual JE, accrual-only landed costs, out-of-sequence reversals, cancelled POs with rounding residuals. Those are the items the suspension and exclusion mechanisms exist to handle. Where JDE Concepts Live in RapidReconciler Quick lookup for users coming in with strong JDE intuition. JDE Concept Where in RapidReconciler PO Receiver table ( F43121 ) The unreconciled view in the PO Receipts module — lists every open Match Type 1 record. Match Type 1 / Match Type 2 status Visible at the order and line level; the suspension/exclusion controls live on the same screens. P4314 voucher match RapidReconciler does not perform the match — it shows you whether the match has happened. The match itself stays in JDE. RNV GL account The Variance Calculation section on the RNV reconciliation page; the GL side of the F43121 vs. GL comparison. Edit Note button The single point of control for applying or reversing both suspensions and exclusions. The checkbox in the popup is the toggle. Manual JEs to RNV Once posted in JDE and imported, they appear as adjustments in the GL side of the variance — and any orphaned F43121 record they cleared becomes a candidate for suspension or exclusion. Cancelled POs / orphaned reversals Show up as open F43121 records in the unreconciled view; each is a candidate for suspension or exclusion depending on whether the issue is whole-order or line-level. Audit Report The permanent record of a period’s reconciliation, including suspended and excluded items with their notes. See PO Receipts → Reference Guide for the deep concept material on F43121, match types, PRLAND, and AAIs. You don’t need to read it now — just know where it is. Try this now If you have RR access, log in and navigate to the PO Receipts module. Open any open PO order and try to change something on it — edit a quantity, change a date, alter a price. You can’t. The fields are read-only. That’s the read-only model in action: every correction you’ll ever make to PO data lands in JD Edwards using the tools you already know. RapidReconciler shows you what needs attention, but doesn’t change anything. The two things you can change are notes and the suspension/exclusion checkboxes via Edit Note. Find the Edit Note button on the same screen so you know where it is — you’ll use it next week.

Your First Session A 10-minute guided first sit-down at the PO Receipts module. The aim is not to do real work — it’s to confirm you can find each landmark and read what it says. If the RNV variance is non-zero at the end, that’s expected. Investigation comes later. Before You Start You need a RapidReconciler login (provided by your administrator) and a browser. JDE access is not required for this session — you’re only looking, not posting. The 10-Minute Walk # Action What To Look For 1 Log in. You land on the default landing page. Navigate to the PO Receipts module from the top navigation. Bookmark this URL for later. 2 Find the status indicators. Confirm that the latest import has succeeded before reviewing data. If anything is red or yellow, hover for a tooltip — do not act on the data until the indicators are green. 3 Open the period selector. Pick the most recently closed period (not the current open one). Closed periods are stable; the current period changes daily as receipts are posted in JDE. 4 Apply your account filter. Filter to the RNV account(s) you’re responsible for. The filter applies left-to-right; removing a company also removes its child entities. 5 Read the RNV variance summary. The headline numbers: the GL balance for the RNV account, the open F43121 total, and the variance between them. Zero variance means F43121 agrees to the GL. 6 Open the unreconciled view. Lists every open Match Type 1 record contributing to the F43121 open total. Don’t investigate yet — just see the shape of what’s open. 7 Drill into one order. Click into any open order. You’ll see the order header, the lines, and the Edit Note button (the control for suspensions and exclusions). Don’t click Edit Note yet — but locate it. 8 Log out, take a break. You’ve seen the page. The next session is when you start asking why a specific F43121 record is open and whether it will ever clear. i If something doesn’t look right — for example, the RNV GL balance differs from JDE by a large amount, or the period selector is empty — stop and ask your administrator. These usually indicate a configuration or permission issue, not a real reconciliation problem. Try this now The 8-step walk above is meant to be done, not just read. If you have RR access, work through the eight steps in your own application before continuing to the next topic. Don’t aim for understanding everything you see — aim for being able to find each landmark. If you got stuck on any step (especially finding the Edit Note button on a specific order), that’s the part to ask your reviewer or administrator about. What You Should Now Be Able To Do After completing the walk above, you should be able to do each of the following on your own. If any of these still feel uncertain, the related step from the walk is worth a second pass. After your first session Log in and reach the PO Receipts module. Confirm the latest import has succeeded before reviewing data. Select a period and apply an account filter for your RNV accounts. Read the RNV variance summary and identify whether F43121 matches the GL. Open the unreconciled view and drill into a specific order. Locate the Edit Note button (without using it yet). See Application Basics for a fuller tour of navigation, search, and the rest of the application.

Your First Week A reading roadmap for week one. The order is procedure-first : open the Walkthrough, follow it, and dip into the Reference Guide only when something stops making sense. JDE experience does the heavy lifting on the conceptual side — reading reference cover-to-cover before doing anything is a slower path to competence. Read These — In This Order # Document Why Now 1 PO Receipts — Reconciliation Walkthrough The end-to-end reconciliation procedure for the RNV account. This is the document you’ll work from day-to-day. Read the Index and Process at a Glance first, then walk the steps in order. 2 Suspensions & Exclusions The mechanism for handling F43121 records that won’t clear through standard processing. Read all of it — this is the part of the job that is genuinely RapidReconciler-specific. Pay special attention to the Decision Guide and the Audit Trail Note examples. 3 PO Receipts — Reference Guide Concept material. Read on demand: when the Walkthrough mentions PRLAND, AAIs, match-type mechanics, or accounting flow and you want more depth, jump here. Don’t try to read straight through. 4 Suspensions & Exclusions — The Audit Trail Note Re-read once you’ve seen real F43121 residuals on screen. The example notes will mean more after you’ve looked at the kind of order they describe. Defer These These documents are excellent but unhelpful in week one. Bookmark them for when you need them. Document Read When Ultimate DMAAI Guide When you’re investigating an RNV variance that traces back to the AAI configuration (typical signal: F43121 records hitting an unexpected GL account). Specific scenario. Suspensions & Exclusions — Best Practices End of month one. Once you’ve applied a few suspensions and exclusions yourself, the operating standards in this section will land harder. Inventory — Reference Guide If a question crosses from RNV into inventory valuation. AP clerks at most sites don’t need this; if you do, the inventory team can usually answer faster than the doc can. Try this now If your environment has any suspended orders or excluded lines, find one and read its audit-trail note. Does the note explain what was done, why it’s acceptable to remove from variance, and reference a JE or ticket number? If yes, that’s a good model for the notes you’ll write. If no — if the note is something like “Cleared by JE” with no further detail — that’s a real example of what not to do, and it’s worth knowing your team has produced these in the past. If your environment has no suspensions or exclusions yet (typical for new deployments), browse a few open F43121 records in the unreconciled view to get a feel for what the items look like before you start making decisions about them. Stop and Ask If… Three signals that mean stop reading and find a colleague or your administrator. None of these are failures — they’re the questions experienced users still ask each other. i The RNV GL balance in RapidReconciler doesn’t match what JDE shows. The numbers come from JDE. Disagreement means your filter, your period, or your account selection is off. Don’t investigate variances until the GL number agrees with JDE. i You’re about to suspend an order but you’re not certain the GL is correct. Suspensions and exclusions remove items from the variance calculation; they don’t fix anything. If the GL is wrong, the JE has to come first. Ask your reviewer before suspending in this situation. i You’re uncertain whether something is a suspension or an exclusion. The decision guide answers this in four questions, but the judgment behind “does this affect the whole order or just one line?” can be ambiguous. When in doubt, prefer an exclusion — it’s the more precise tool and it leaves more activity visible in the standard view.

Your First Period-End Close The transition from monitoring RNV variances to closing and posting. The procedure is fully documented in the PO Receipts Reconciliation Walkthrough . This topic covers the things around the procedure: how most sites approach a first close, and the three mistakes that bite new AP users hardest. Two Recommendations Before You Close Live Neither of these is enforced by the application. Both are worth the time. Pair With an Experienced Reviewer for the First Close Have someone who has closed before sit with you (or be on a call) for your first end-to-end period close. Their job is not to do the work — it is to catch the small judgment calls that the documentation can’t capture (in particular, suspension-vs-exclusion decisions on real orders, and the materiality threshold your team applies). Most failures on a first close are not procedural mistakes; they are confidence mistakes. Walk a Closed Prior Period Before Closing Live Before your first live close, walk through a closed prior period in the application without changing anything. Look at the orders that were suspended or excluded in that close, read the audit-trail notes, and confirm you understand why each decision was made. The reasoning behind suspension and exclusion choices is the part of the job that doesn’t come from JDE experience — seeing how your team has handled it before is the fastest way to internalize the local standard. If a non-production environment is available, even better: re-run the close in non-production, applying suspensions and exclusions yourself, and compare your decisions to what was actually done. Pre-Close Checklist Before you post the closing journal entry, confirm the following: Pre-close requirements The latest import has succeeded; status indicators are green. All open F43121 records that should clear through standard voucher matching have done so — AP has worked through the in-flight match queue. Each remaining unresolvable F43121 record has been reviewed and either suspended or excluded with a defensible audit-trail note. The aggregate suspension and exclusion totals tie to the GL clearing JE you’re about to post. Any prior-period suspensions intended as temporary (cross-period timing) have been reviewed for reversal. Top Three New-User Mistakes Drawn from the Best Practices and Pitfalls sections of the existing PO Receipts docs, narrowed to the three that specifically catch first-time closers. ! 1. Suspending an order to hide a real problem. A suspension is an annotation, not a correction. If the GL is wrong, fix the GL first — suspending the order won’t fix the underlying issue and will make next month’s reconciliation harder. The test: if you can’t write a defensible note explaining why removing this item from the variance is appropriate, you’re probably suspending too soon. ! 2. Vague audit-trail notes. “Cleared by JE” or “Per accounting” will not survive an audit review six months later. Every note must say what the issue is, why removing the item is acceptable, the JE or ticket reference, and the date. The example notes in Suspensions & Exclusions → Audit Trail Note show what good looks like. ! 3. Not reviewing prior suspensions for new activity. If you suspend a whole order whose OT is still open in JDE, future receipts can land against it — and because the order is suspended, those receipts will not appear in the variance calculation. Each period-end close should include a check of previously-suspended orders for new activity. If new activity is found, unexclude/re-exclude is the standard correction. If Your First Close Has a Non-Zero Variance After Posting It happens. The Audit Report tells you what was suspended, what was excluded, and what JEs were posted. Review it with your buddy. Most causes fall into one of these buckets: An F43121 record contributed to the variance that should have been suspended or excluded but wasn’t. A suspension or exclusion was applied but the matching JE was not posted (or vice versa). The post-JE nightly refresh hadn’t completed when the variance was checked — check again the next morning. New voucher activity posted in JDE between the close and the import. None of these is a failure of the close itself. They’re learning events. Document what happened in your team’s notes — the second close benefits enormously from the first one’s post-mortem. See PO Receipts Reconciliation Walkthrough for the full close procedure and the complete pitfalls list.

Self-Check Six scenarios to test your reading. Read each one, decide what you’d do, and click Where to find this to confirm. If a scenario surprises you or you’d guess wrong, that’s the topic to re-read — not a failure. The questions are deliberately scenario-based rather than fact-based; the suspension/exclusion decisions in this job reward judgment more than rule recall. Scenario 1 A purchase order has 5 lines. Lines 1, 2, and 3 vouchered cleanly through P4314. Line 4 was cleared by a manual JE because the supplier billed it on a separate invoice. Line 5 has an orphaned reversal that IT confirms cannot be re-matched. Suspend the whole order, exclude lines 4 and 5 individually, or some combination? Why? Where to find this: Suspensions & Exclusions → Decision Guide Scenario 2 You’re about to suspend an order. You suspect the GL might be wrong but you haven’t verified it yet. The deadline is approaching and suspending the order would clear the variance from the headline number. Is it acceptable to suspend now and verify the GL later? What does the doc recommend, and why? Where to find this: Your First Period-End Close → Top three new-user mistakes Scenario 3 You’re writing the audit-trail note for a suspension. Your draft reads: “Cleared by JE.” What’s missing? Name at least three elements a defensible note should include. Where to find this: Suspensions & Exclusions → The Audit Trail Note Scenario 4 Three months ago, an admin suspended an entire PO because all its receipts had been cleared by JE. This month, you notice the order is still suspended — but JDE shows new receipt activity has posted against the same OT. Is the new activity contributing to the variance calculation? What do you do? Where to find this: Your First Period-End Close → Top three new-user mistakes Scenario 5 You see an open F43121 record from a receipt dated 12/29 against an order that was vouchered 1/3 in the next period. The auditor has flagged it as a variance for the December close. Should you suspend or exclude this for the December close? If yes, what should you do in January? Where to find this: Suspensions & Exclusions → When to Use Each (Cross-Period Timing) Scenario 6 You’re uncertain whether the situation in front of you calls for a suspension or an exclusion. The order has multiple lines, but the issue might affect more than one of them — you can’t tell yet. When in doubt, which mechanism does the doc recommend, and why? Where to find this: Suspensions & Exclusions → Decision Guide (Rule of thumb) i How to use this. The questions don’t have a score. They’re a self-test for whether the doc landed. If three or more felt uncertain, the doc didn’t land and is worth a re-read — ideally with the specific topic each scenario points to. If they all felt obvious, you’re ready to start working real cases.

Vocabulary RapidReconciler-specific terms that aren’t standard JDE, with focus on the PO Receipts module. Each entry is a one-line definition plus a link to the doc that explains it in depth. Skim once for awareness; come back when a term shows up on screen and you want to remember what it meant. Audit-trail note The note entered through the Edit Note popup that documents why a suspension or exclusion was applied. Required — an annotation without context cannot be defended in audit. Suspensions & Exclusions → The Audit Trail Note . Edit Note button The single point of control for both applying and reversing suspensions and exclusions. The same button handles both actions; the checkbox in the popup is the toggle. Exclusion A line-level annotation that removes a single PO line from the variance calculation while leaving the rest of the order visible. Used when the issue is contained to one line on an otherwise active order. Suspensions vs. Exclusions . Exclusion (In Transit module) Different from PO Receipts exclusion. In the In Transit module, an exclusion applies at the order-pair level (ST/OT) and clears unbalanced transfer order pairs. Same word, different mechanism. Transfer Order Exclusion Guide . F43121 The JD Edwards PO Receiver table — the source of truth for open receipts. RapidReconciler reads from it on every nightly import and never modifies it. Standard JDE term, included here because it’s the central object of the RNV reconciliation. Match Type 1 / Match Type 2 Match Type 1 = receipt record. Match Type 2 = voucher record. In a clean reconciliation, every Match Type 1 is offset by a Match Type 2; orphaned Match Type 1 records are the typical candidates for suspension or exclusion. RNV (Received-Not-Vouchered) The clearing GL account that holds value for goods received but not yet vouchered. The reconciliation goal is to make this account balance to F43121’s open total. Suspension An order-level annotation that removes an entire PO from the variance calculation. Used when the whole order is non-actionable — for example, when a JE has cleared the entire RNV residual and the F43121 records are orphaned. Suspensions vs. Exclusions . Unexclude / Re-exclude procedure The way to handle a previously-excluded line that has had new activity. Remove the exclusion, review the new activity, then re-apply the exclusion. Applying & Reversing . Unreconciled view The default view in the PO Receipts module — lists every open Match Type 1 record contributing to the F43121 open total. Suspended orders and excluded lines are removed from this view (and have their own filtered views). Variance Calculation section The breakdown of the RNV variance into attributable sources, including a distinct line for the aggregate of suspensions and exclusions. The number on that line is the value of the offsetting JE you’ll post in JDE. Back to Index for the milestones and topic table.

Start Here — Inventory A wayfinding guide for inventory accountants and cost accountants new to RapidReconciler. JD Edwards experience is assumed throughout. Warehouse personnel responsible for transfer orders should read Start Here — Warehouse Transfers instead. Who This Is For You are responsible for some or all of the inventory reconciliation cycle in JD Edwards: monitoring variances during the period, investigating cardex and account discrepancies, and posting closing entries at period end. RapidReconciler is the tool that surfaces what needs your attention and tells you where to look in JDE. This guide does not teach JDE. It assumes you already know what F4111 is, what a DMAAI does, and how a perpetual inventory system reconciles to the GL. What it does is map that JDE knowledge to RapidReconciler’s vocabulary, screens, and workflow — and tell you what to read, in what order, in your first month. What “Ready” Looks Like Use these milestones to gauge your own progress. They are not prescriptive timelines — some sites move faster, some take longer — but they are the typical learning arc for a JDE-experienced inventory user. Day 1 Logged in. Both status lights green. Can identify your accounts in the filter and select the period you care about. Week 1 Can read the Valuation section and explain what each variance source (Carry Forward, GL Batches, End of Day, Transactions, Cardex, Manual JEs) means and where it comes from. Month 1 Has run a full period-end close at least once with supervision. Has produced and saved an Audit Report. Has reviewed Integrity Reports 2 through 6. Topics Topic Read When What RapidReconciler Does First. Five minutes that map JDE concepts to where you’ll find them in RapidReconciler. Your First Session Day 1. A 10-minute guided first sit-down at the Reconciliation page. Your First Week Week 1. The four documents to read — in order — and which ones to defer. Your First Period-End Close When your first close is on the calendar. The buddy-system and mock-close recommendations, plus the top three new-user mistakes. Self-Check End of week 1. Six scenario questions to test whether the doc landed. Not graded. Vocabulary Anytime. RapidReconciler-specific terms that aren’t standard JDE. i Recommended at most sites. Pair with an experienced reviewer for your first period-end close, and run one mock close against a prior period in a non-production environment before the live one. Neither is enforced by the application — but both noticeably reduce first-close stress. i Key principle. RapidReconciler is read-only against JDE. It tells you what is out of balance and where to look — but every correction lands in JD Edwards. If you’re ever unsure whether to act, the answer is “investigate first, post second.”

What RapidReconciler Does (and Doesn’t) Five minutes of orientation. The aim is to translate what you already know about JDE into where it lives and what it’s called inside RapidReconciler — and to set boundaries on what RapidReconciler can and cannot change. The Read-Only Model RapidReconciler does not write to JDE. It reads from JDE on a nightly import cycle, compares the data, and surfaces discrepancies. Every correction — every IA, every JE, every DMAAI fix — is posted in JD Edwards using the tools you already know. RapidReconciler then picks up the change at the next nightly refresh. RapidReconciler can change Only JD Edwards can change Notes attached to a transaction or order; suspensions and exclusions on PO Receipts; filter and view preferences; report exports. F4111, F41021, F0902, F0911, DMAAI tables, item master records, GL account balances — everything that matters for a reconciliation. The practical implication: if you spot a problem in RapidReconciler at 2pm and post a corrective JE in JDE at 3pm, the variance will not clear in RapidReconciler until the following morning’s import. Plan investigations and corrections accordingly. What It’s Comparing The headline question RapidReconciler answers for inventory is one you already know how to ask: does the perpetual ledger agree to the general ledger for this period? RapidReconciler Field JDE Source What It Represents GL Balance F0902 The general ledger period-end balance for the inventory account. Perpetual Balance F4111 summarized Sum of every cardex transaction posted to that account in that period. Out of Balance Calculated The difference. Zero means the perpetual agrees to the GL. If the Out of Balance is non-zero, the Variance Calculation section breaks the difference into six attributable sources (Carry Forward, GL Batches, End of Day, Transactions, Cardex, Manual JEs). Each one points to a specific kind of JDE-side correction. Most of your day-to-day work will be working those six lines down to zero. Where JDE Concepts Live in RapidReconciler Quick lookup for users coming in with strong JDE intuition. JDE Concept Where in RapidReconciler Item ledger detail ( F4111 ) As-Of page; Cardex Integrity pop-up. On-hand balance ( F41021 ) Compared automatically to F4111 in the Cardex Integrity pop-up. DMAAI table 4152 Inventory Reference Guide and Ultimate DMAAI Guide; Integrity Reports 2 and 3. GL account / class code Account filter (Company → Business Unit → Object → Subsidiary); Integrity Report 5. Period-end close Reconciliation page Variance Calculation, Audit Report. Manual JEs to inventory Manual Journal Entries variance line on the Reconciliation page. Cost rolls / frozen vs. average Cardex Integrity pop-up; Integrity Report 6 (Frozen Cost). UOM conversions As-Of page (look for -9999 ); Integrity Report 4. See Inventory → Reference Guide for the deep concept material. You don’t need to read it now — just know where it is when something on the screen surprises you. Try this now If you have RR access, log in and find the Reconciliation page. Pick one of your accounts and one closed period. Compare the GL Balance in RR to the same account and period in JDE. They should match exactly — if they don’t, your filter or period is off, not the data. You’re not investigating anything yet — just confirming that the read-only model behaves the way this topic just described.

Your First Session A 10-minute guided first sit-down at the Reconciliation page. The aim is not to do real work — it’s to confirm you can find each landmark and read what it says. If your Out of Balance is non-zero at the end, that’s expected. Investigation comes later. Before You Start You need a RapidReconciler login (provided by your administrator) and a browser. JDE access is not required for this session — you’re only looking, not posting. The 10-Minute Walk # Action What To Look For 1 Log in. You land on the Reconciliation page by default. Bookmark this URL for later. 2 Find the two status lights. Top center of the page. Inventory Validation on the left, System Status on the right. Both should be green. If either is red or yellow, hover for a tooltip explaining why — do not act on the data until both are green. 3 Open the period selector. Top right corner. Pick the most recently closed period (not the current open one). Closed periods are stable; the current period changes daily. 4 Apply your account filter. Left side of the page, four columns: Company → Business Unit → Object → Subsidiary . Select the company and account combination you’re responsible for. The filter applies left-to-right; removing a company also removes its child entities. 5 Read the Valuation section. Three numbers: GL Balance , Perpetual Balance , Out of Balance . The GL Balance should match what you see in JDE for the same account and period. If it doesn’t, your filter or period is off — the numbers come from JDE, so they will agree if you’re asking the same question. 6 Glance at the Variance Calculation section. Below Valuation. Six lines, one per variance source. The non-zero ones are where the Out of Balance comes from. Don’t investigate yet — just see them. 7 Click the Out of Balance History Graph. Shows variance trends across the last 14 periods. Tells you at a glance whether your accounts run clean or have a chronic issue. Click a data point to jump to that period. 8 Log out, take a break. You’ve seen the page. The next session is when you start asking why the variance lines are non-zero. i If something doesn’t look right — for example, the GL Balance differs from JDE by a large amount, or the period selector is empty — stop and ask your administrator. These usually indicate a configuration or permission issue, not a real reconciliation problem. Try this now The 10-minute walk above is meant to be done, not just read. If you have RR access, work through the eight steps in your own application before continuing to the next topic. Don’t aim for understanding everything you see — aim for being able to find each landmark. If you got stuck on any step, that’s the part to ask your administrator about. What You Should Now Be Able To Do After completing the walk above, you should be able to do each of the following on your own. If any of these still feel uncertain, the related step from the walk is worth a second pass. After your first session Log in and reach the Reconciliation page. Confirm both status lights are green before reviewing data. Select a period and apply an account filter that matches your responsibilities. Read the Valuation section and identify whether perpetual matches the GL. Locate the Variance Calculation section even if you can’t yet investigate it. See Application Basics if you want a fuller tour of navigation, search, and the rest of the application before going further.

Your First Week A reading roadmap for week one. The order is procedure-first : open the Walkthrough, follow it, and dip into the Reference Guide only when something stops making sense. JDE experience does the heavy lifting on the conceptual side — reading reference cover-to-cover before doing anything is a slower path to competence. Read These — In This Order # Document Why Now 1 Inventory Reconciliation Walkthrough — Index + Process at a Glance The flowchart on Process at a Glance is your mental model for the whole job. Five minutes here saves hours later. 2 Reconciliation Walkthrough — Step 1 Status lights and filters. You did most of this in Your First Session ; this is the formal version with the edge cases. 3 Reconciliation Walkthrough — Step 2 The biggest topic in the Walkthrough. Read all six variance sources even if some of your accounts don’t exhibit them — you need to recognize each one when it appears. 4 Inventory — Reference Guide Concept material. Read on demand: when Step 2 mentions DMAAI, period-end logic, or GL class codes and you want more depth, jump here. Don’t try to read straight through. The Five Variance Sources at a Glance Each non-zero line in the Variance Calculation section corresponds to a different kind of discrepancy with its own corrective procedure. This table is a routing map: when you see a non-zero variance on your account, it tells you what the source likely means and where to find the full procedure for clearing it. The table doesn’t replace reading the procedure — it points you at the right one. Variance Source What It Likely Means Where to Find the Procedure Carry Forward A prior-period variance was left unresolved. The amount carried into this period and is still open. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 2.2 (Carry Forward) GL Batches One or more GL batches haven’t posted — usually approval holds, posting errors, or missing batch headers. Must be $0 before close. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 2.2 (GL Batches) End of Day Item ledger records exist but no GL batch has been created yet — usually because nightly Sales Update or Manufacturing Accounting jobs haven’t run. Must be $0 before close. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 2.2 (End of Day) Transactions Dollar differences between item ledger and GL entries that didn’t match on company, account, period, or document. The corrective action is always an offsetting manual JE in JDE. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 2.2 (Transactions) Cardex Items where the summarized item ledger doesn’t match the on-hand balance. Either a quantity variance (needs IT SQL correction) or an amount variance (cleared via dollars-only IA in JDE). Handling Cardex Variance — the dedicated walkthrough i About Manual Journal Entries. A sixth line on the Variance Calculation section, Manual Journal Entries , surfaces manual entries posted directly to the inventory GL account. This is informational rather than investigational — the line shows that manual entries are accounted for in the variance math, not that something is wrong. No corrective action. Defer These These documents are excellent but unhelpful in week one. Bookmark them for when you need them. Document Read When Reconciliation Walkthrough — Step 3 When your first period-end close is on the calendar (covered in Your First Period-End Close ). Reconciliation Walkthrough — Step 4 Same. Step 4 is the integrity-report review that runs alongside Step 3 at period end. Handling Cardex Variance When the Cardex line in the Variance Calculation goes non-zero on one of your accounts. Don’t pre-read — the procedure won’t stick without a real example to anchor it. Ultimate DMAAI Guide When you’re investigating a Transactions-line variance and the Reference Guide’s DMAAI section isn’t enough. Most users never need this. The ones who do, need it deeply. Zero Balance Adjustments When you encounter an item with a value but zero quantity on the As-Of page. Specific scenario; learn it when it appears. About Units of Measure When Integrity Report 4 flags an item, or when an As-Of quantity shows -9999 . Specific scenario. Try this now Pick one of your accounts in RR. Open the Variance Calculation section for the most recently closed period and identify which of the six lines (Carry Forward, GL Batches, End of Day, Transactions, Cardex, Manual JEs) are non-zero. You don’t need to investigate them yet. The goal is to see — on your own data — what variance sources actually look like in your environment. Some accounts run clean every period; others have a chronic Carry Forward or Cardex line. Knowing which is which for your accounts shapes how the rest of your week unfolds. Stop and Ask If… Three signals that mean stop reading and find a colleague or your administrator. None of these are failures — they’re the questions experienced users still ask each other. i The GL Balance in RapidReconciler doesn’t match what JDE shows. The numbers come from JDE. Disagreement means your filter, your period, or your account selection is off. Don’t investigate variances until the GL number agrees with JDE. i The status light is yellow and won’t go green. A flashing yellow System Status means the import is still running. If it’s been running for hours, contact your administrator — this is an operational issue, not a user one. i You can’t see the accounts you expected. Account permissions are configured per user. If your filter list is missing a company or business unit you should see, ask your administrator before working around it.

Your First Period-End Close The transition from monitoring variances to closing and posting. The procedure is fully documented in the Reconciliation Walkthrough — Step 3 and Step 4 . This topic covers the things around the procedure: how most sites approach a first close, and the three mistakes that bite new users hardest. Two Recommendations Before You Close Live Neither of these is enforced by the application. Both are worth the time. Pair With an Experienced Reviewer for the First Close Have someone who has closed before sit with you (or be on a call) for your first end-to-end period close. Their job is not to do the work — it is to catch the small judgment calls that the documentation can’t capture, and to confirm you’re reading what you think you’re reading. Most failures on a first close are not procedural mistakes; they are confidence mistakes (acting on data that isn’t ready, or hesitating on data that is). Run One Mock Close in a Non-Production Environment If your organization has a non-production RapidReconciler environment with reasonably current data, run a complete close against a prior period before doing it live. Walk through Steps 1 through 4 end-to-end, post a mock JE, produce the Audit Report. Going through the motions once with no consequences is the single highest-leverage thing you can do to be ready for a real close. If a non-production environment isn’t available, the next best option is to walk through a closed prior period in production with your reviewer — reading what was done without changing anything — so you understand what a successful close looks like before producing your own. Pre-Close Checklist Before you post the closing journal entry, the four pre-close conditions in Walkthrough Step 3.1 must be satisfied. Repeating them here for emphasis: Pre-close requirements GL Batches variance = $0. End of Day variance = $0. Transaction variances reviewed; offsetting JEs prepared. Carry Forward accounted for in the manual entry (if applicable). If any of those four are not met, do not proceed. The Walkthrough explains why each one matters. Top Three New-User Mistakes Drawn from the Pitfalls section of the Reconciliation Walkthrough, narrowed to the three that specifically catch first-time closers. ! 1. Posting on stale data. If either status light is red or yellow, the data is not ready. Acting on it bakes a variance into the period that you’ll have to chase next month as Carry Forward. The single most common new-user mistake is treating “the lights will go green soon” as a green light. Wait. ! 2. Skipping Carry Forward investigation because the amount is small. A small Carry Forward this month becomes a slightly larger Carry Forward next month, and the cause becomes harder to trace each period. Resolve in the period it originates whenever possible. If you accept it into your manual JE for materiality reasons, document the decision in the audit trail — future-you will need to know. ! 3. Not saving the Audit Report externally. The Audit Report is the permanent record of the period’s reconciliation. RapidReconciler will not retain detail data forever — purges happen on schedule. After every close, export the Audit Report to your team’s document repository and confirm it’s there before considering the close complete. If Your First Close Has a Non-Zero Out of Balance After Posting It happens. The Audit Report tells you what was posted and what the balances looked like at the moment of posting. Review it with your buddy. Most causes fall into one of four buckets: A variance line was non-zero at post time and got rolled into the JE rather than being investigated. The post-JE nightly refresh hadn’t completed when the Out of Balance was checked — check again the next morning. An additional transaction posted in JDE between the close and the import, creating new activity outside the closed period. Carry Forward from the prior period wasn’t accounted for in the closing JE. None of these is a failure of the close itself. They’re learning events. Document what happened in your team’s notes — the second close benefits enormously from the first one’s post-mortem. See Inventory Reconciliation Walkthrough for the full close procedure (Steps 3 and 4) and the complete Pitfalls list.

Self-Check Six scenarios to test your reading. Read each one, decide what you’d do, and click Where to find this to confirm. If a scenario surprises you or you’d guess wrong, that’s the topic to re-read — not a failure. The questions are deliberately scenario-based rather than fact-based; the job they reflect is judgment, not recall. Scenario 1 You log in. The Reconciliation page shows GL Balance $1,250,000 for January, but JDE shows $1,275,000 for the same account and period. What’s the most likely cause, and what would you do first? Where to find this: Your First Week → Stop and ask if… Scenario 2 An end-user tells you the System Status light is yellow but they need to close the period today — the deadline is in two hours. What’s the right response: post anyway, wait, or call the administrator? Where to find this: Your First Period-End Close → Top three new-user mistakes Scenario 3 The Variance Calculation section shows: Carry Forward $48, GL Batches $0, End of Day $0, Transactions $0, Cardex $0, Manual JEs $1,200. Which lines, if any, prevent you from posting the closing JE? Which lines are informational? Where to find this: What RapidReconciler Does → What it’s comparing Scenario 4 You closed the period yesterday. Today, the Out of Balance shows $230 for the closed period. Yesterday it was $0. What are the four most likely explanations, and which one is most common? Where to find this: Your First Period-End Close → If your first close has a non-zero Out of Balance after posting Scenario 5 You see a small Carry Forward amount ($85) on your account this period. You investigate briefly and can’t find an obvious cause. The deadline is approaching. Roll it into the closing JE, or hold the close and keep investigating? What does the doc recommend? Where to find this: Your First Period-End Close → Top three new-user mistakes Scenario 6 You’re trying to filter to a specific business unit but it doesn’t appear in the filter list. You know it exists in JDE. Is this a configuration issue, a permission issue, a JDE issue, or a user error? Who do you ask? Where to find this: Your First Week → Stop and ask if… i How to use this. The questions don’t have a score. They’re a self-test for whether the doc landed. If three or more felt uncertain, the doc didn’t land and is worth a re-read — ideally with the specific topic each scenario points to. If they all felt obvious, you’re ready to move on to your first session.

Vocabulary RapidReconciler-specific terms that aren’t standard JDE. Each entry is a one-line definition plus a link to the doc that explains it in depth. Skim once for awareness; come back when a term shows up on screen and you want to remember what it meant. As-Of page The supporting detail behind the Perpetual Balance — lets you view item/branch/location/lot balances at any single date in the period. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Reference Material . Audit Report The permanent record of a period’s reconciliation. Contains accounts summary, unposted batches, end-of-day items, manual JEs, variances, and perpetual detail. Save externally after every close. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 3.3 . Cardex Integrity pop-up The view that lists items where summarized F4111 doesn’t agree to F41021 . Tells you whether the variance is quantity, amount, or both. Handling Cardex Variance . Carry Forward variance The portion of this period’s Out of Balance that was unresolved in the prior period. If left untreated it accumulates and compounds over time. End of Day variance Item ledger records ( F4111 ) that don’t yet have a batch number or GL date because Sales Update or Manufacturing Accounting hasn’t run. Must be $0 before period close. GL Batches variance Unposted GL detail entries ( F0911 ). Must be $0 before period close. Integrity Reports (2–6) Six monthly-review reports surfacing JDE configuration issues that cause future variances: DMAAI Entry, Excluded GL Classes, UOM Conversion, GL Class Integrity, Frozen Cost. Reviewed monthly, not just when something is wrong. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 4 . Manual Journal Entries (variance line) Manual entries posted directly to the inventory GL account, surfaced in the variance calculation so they’re visible inside the reconciliation. No corrective action required — the line is informational. Offset Account widget (star icon) Period-end close tool. Hover over End of Day or Transactions to see the inventory account and suggested offset for each variance, exportable to Excel for pasting into a JDE JE. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Step 2.3 . Out of Balance The headline number on the Reconciliation page. GL Balance minus Perpetual Balance. Zero means the perpetual agrees to the GL for the selected period and accounts. Out of Balance History Graph The 14-period trend chart on the Reconciliation page. The fastest way to tell whether a variance is recurring or a one-off. Reconciliation page The default landing page after login. Status lights, period selector, account filters, Valuation section, and Variance Calculation section all live here. Roll Forward page Item activity across a date range, mapped to administrator-configured columns. Informational — not part of the standard reconciliation flow. Reconciliation Walkthrough → Reference Material . Status lights Two indicators at the top center of the Reconciliation page. Inventory Validation confirms prior-period carry-forward is accurate; System Status confirms the latest JDE import succeeded. Both must be green before posting any GL adjustments. Tolerance Adjust A line on the Offset Account export representing sub-unit rounding. Replace with the correct GL account before pasting into JDE. Transactions page Used when the Transactions line in the Variance Calculation is non-zero. Lists documents where F4111 doesn’t match F0911 . Reconciliation Walkthrough → Reference Material . Valuation section The three-number block on the Reconciliation page: GL Balance, Perpetual Balance, Out of Balance. Answers “does perpetual match the GL for this period?” Variance Calculation section Below Valuation. Six attributable lines (Carry Forward, GL Batches, End of Day, Transactions, Cardex, Manual JEs). The non-zero ones are where Out of Balance comes from. Back to Index for the milestones and topic table.

Start Here — Warehouse Transfers A wayfinding guide for everyone whose work touches In-Transit reconciliation in RapidReconciler — from warehouse personnel who ship and receive between branches to the inventory accountants who clear variances at period close. JD Edwards experience is assumed throughout. i Module availability. If you can’t find the In Transit module in your main navigation, contact your RapidReconciler administrator. They will either grant you access (if the module is already enabled at your site) or open a request with GSI support to enable it. Who This Is For This guide serves two distinct roles, often filled by different people at the same customer site. Topics are tagged so you can read only what applies to you — or read everything if you wear both hats. If your job is… Read Warehouse Shipping or receiving transfers between branches. Getting Oriented topics + Your Daily Practice + Common Mistakes. About 10 minutes total. The Self-Check at the end has scenarios for both roles. Inventory Accountant Investigating In-Transit variances at period close. Everything. The warehouse topics give you context on what your data looked like before it reached you — useful for understanding why certain variances appear. What “Ready” Looks Like Use these milestones to gauge your own progress. Inventory accountant milestones in the third column don’t apply to warehouse-only readers. Day 1 Logged in. Can find the In Transit module. Can locate a specific transfer order pair by ST or OT number. Week 1 Can read TranQty and TranAmt for a pair and explain whether goods are still in transit, fully received, or have a variance. Month 1 (inventory accountant) Has run a period-end close on In Transit at least once with supervision. Has applied at least one exclusion with a defensible audit-trail note. Topics Topic For Read When What RapidReconciler Does Everyone First. Five minutes mapping JDE concepts to where you’ll find them in RR. Order Pairs Explained Everyone First. The foundational concept — what an order pair is and why it matters. Your Daily Practice Warehouse How to use RR to confirm your transfers cleared and when to flag something. Common Mistakes Warehouse The top errors that cause In-Transit variances and how to prevent them. Your First Week Inventory Accountant The four documents to read — in order — and which to defer. Your First Period-End Close Inventory Accountant When period-end is on the calendar. Buddy and mock-close recommendations, plus the top three new-reviewer mistakes. The Exclusion Decision Inventory Accountant When to exclude a pair, when not to, and what the consequences are. Self-Check Everyone End of week 1. Six scenario questions, role-tagged. Not graded. Vocabulary Everyone Anytime. RapidReconciler-specific terms relevant to In-Transit work. i Recommended at most sites. Inventory accountants should pair with an experienced reviewer for the first period-end close, and walk a closed prior period before doing one live. Warehouse personnel benefit from a 15-minute walkthrough with someone who has handled exception cases (cost variances, mismatched receipts) before they encounter their first one. Neither is enforced — both noticeably reduce stress. i Key principle. RapidReconciler is read-only against JD Edwards. Every correction — whether you’re posting a missed receipt, fixing a quantity, or adjusting a cost — happens in JDE. RR shows you what needs attention and tells you where to look. Related Documentation Document Relevance In Transit Reconciliation Walkthrough The full procedural reference for In-Transit reconciliation. Inventory accountants will live in this doc. Transfer Order Exclusion Guide Deep dive on the exclusion mechanism — when to use it, how to apply it, and how to reverse it. Transfer Order Reference Guide JDE-side concepts: ST/OT order types, the F4211 record structure, related order linkage. Application Basics Login, navigation, and application overview.

What RapidReconciler Does (and Doesn’t) Five minutes of orientation. The aim is to translate what you already know about JD Edwards transfer orders into where it lives and what it’s called inside RapidReconciler — and to set boundaries on what RR can and cannot change. For everyone. Both warehouse personnel and inventory accountants benefit from this orientation. Skip nothing. The Read-Only Model RapidReconciler does not write to JDE. It reads from JDE on a nightly import cycle, compares the data, and surfaces discrepancies. Every correction — every receipt entry, every quantity adjustment, every JE — is posted in JD Edwards using the tools you already know. RapidReconciler then picks up the change at the next nightly refresh. RapidReconciler can change Only JD Edwards can change Notes attached to an order pair; exclusions on a pair; filter and view preferences; report exports. F4211 (sales orders), F4311/F43121 (purchase orders and receipts), F0902 (GL balances), shipment and receipt records, item master — everything that matters for transfer reconciliation. The practical implication: if a missed receipt is posted in JDE at 3pm, RR won’t reflect the receipt until the next morning’s import. The exclusion mechanism (which is in RR) is what removes a pair from the variance calculation in the meantime if it’s known to be unresolvable. What It’s Comparing The headline question RapidReconciler answers for In Transit is: does the sum of all open transfer order pair values agree to the In-Transit GL clearing account balance? In a clean environment, every shipment (ST) is eventually offset by a matching receipt (OT), the order pair’s TranQty and TranAmt fall to zero, and that pair stops contributing to the In-Transit balance. In practice, certain pairs never clear — cost variances between branches, items shipped against the wrong OT, partial receipts that nobody completed, transfers cancelled mid-flight. Those are the items the inventory accountant investigates. Where JDE Concepts Live in RapidReconciler Quick lookup for users coming in with strong JDE intuition. JDE Concept Where in RapidReconciler Sales order detail ( F4211 ) — the ST Shipping side of every order pair on the Orders page. PO detail / receipts ( F4311 / F43121 ) — the OT Receiving side of every order pair on the Orders page. Related order linkage How RR matches the ST to the OT into a single row. In-Transit GL clearing account The Reconciliation page Valuation section. Item-level branch balances The In Transit As-Of page. Manual JEs to In Transit Manual Journal Entries variance line on the Reconciliation page. Document and order types RR detects ST/OT pairings via a flag in F4211; specific order type codes are configurable per environment. See Transfer Order Reference Guide for the deep concept material on JDE-side mechanics. You don’t need to read it now — just know where it is. Try this now If you have RR access, log in and navigate to the In Transit module. Pick any open order pair and try to change something on it — edit a quantity, change a date, alter a price. You can’t. The fields are read-only. That’s the read-only model in action: every correction lands in JD Edwards. The two things you can change are notes and the exclusion checkbox via the Edit Note popup.

Order Pairs Explained The single most important concept in In-Transit reconciliation. If you understand order pairs, almost everything else on the screen follows. If you don’t, the rest of the doc will feel arbitrary. For everyone. Warehouse personnel use this to verify their transfers; inventory accountants use it to investigate variances. Same concept, different uses. What Is an Order Pair? In JD Edwards, a transfer between two branches creates two separate orders: an ST sales order on the source branch (the goods leaving) and an OT purchase order on the destination branch (the goods arriving). RapidReconciler reads both, matches them using the related order linkage on the F4211 record, and presents them as a single row in the In Transit module. Aspect ST (shipping side) OT (receiving side) JDE table F4211 (sales order detail) F4311 / F43121 (PO detail / receipts) Branch Source — where goods leave from Destination — where goods arrive Records The shipment The receipt Result One ST + One OT + One Item = One row in the In Transit module The pair is summarized at the item number level . If a single transfer moves multiple items, each item produces its own pair — one row per ST/OT/item combination. The Two Numbers That Matter Once paired, RapidReconciler calculates the in-transit position for each pair by subtracting receipts from shipments: Field Calculation What It Means TranQty Ship Qty − Rec Qty Quantity still in transit between the two branches. TranAmt Ship Amt − Rec Amt Dollar value still sitting in the In Transit clearing account. When TranQty and TranAmt are both zero, the pair is fully cleared — quantity shipped equals quantity received, and the dollar value has cleared the In-Transit account. Any non-zero value means there’s open transfer activity contributing to the In-Transit balance. The Three States of an Order Pair For quick mental reference, every pair you encounter is in one of these three states: State TranQty What It Means Cleared = 0 (and TranAmt = 0) Goods received as expected. Pair is no longer contributing to In-Transit balance. Warehouse — this is what success looks like. Open (in flight) > 0 Goods shipped but not yet received. Normal between shipment and receipt — if the gap is days rather than weeks, this is fine. Variance = 0 but TranAmt ≠ 0 Quantity matched but dollars don’t. Cost difference between source and destination — the pair will never clear without action. Inventory Accountant needs to investigate. Why This Matters The In-Transit balance is the sum of every open pair’s TranAmt. The total at the bottom of the Orders page rolls up directly into the headline In-Transit balance shown on the Reconciliation page. Exclusions happen at the pair level. When a reconciler excludes a pair, they’re telling RapidReconciler to stop counting that one ST/OT/item combination toward the In-Transit balance. Cost variances surface at the pair level. If the ST sales price doesn’t equal the OT purchase cost on the same item, the pair will show TranQty = 0 but TranAmt ≠ 0 — a quantity match with a dollar mismatch. Open pairs are the source of every variance. Whatever shows up on the Reconciliation page can be traced back to one or more open pairs on the Orders page. i Note on terminology. Although this guide refers to ST/OT order types, RapidReconciler detects different transfer types based on a flag in the F4211 table. The pairing logic is the same regardless of the specific order type codes used in your environment.

Your Daily Practice How RapidReconciler fits into a typical day for warehouse personnel who ship or receive transfers between branches. The aim isn’t to make you a reconciler — it’s to give you enough RR awareness to confirm your work and to flag the things that need someone else’s attention. For warehouse personnel. Inventory accountants should still skim this topic — understanding what warehouse staff see (and don’t) explains many of the variances you’ll investigate. Where Your Work Shows Up Every shipment and every receipt you process in JD Edwards eventually appears in RapidReconciler the morning after the nightly import. Specifically: When you ship — goods leave the source branch, the ST sales order updates, and the pair’s TranQty (still in transit) increases. Until the matching receipt is processed, the dollars sit in the In-Transit GL account. When you receive — goods arrive at the destination branch, the OT purchase order updates, and the pair’s TranQty drops back toward zero. When TranQty reaches zero and TranAmt also reaches zero, the pair has fully cleared.
