When something goes wrong — partial receipt, wrong OT receipted against, cost difference between branches — the pair stays open in RR until somebody investigates it. What to Do When a Transfer Is Complete For most transfers, no RR action is needed — the pair clears automatically when the receipt posts in JDE. But verifying that the pair actually cleared is worth ten seconds, and saves the inventory accountant from chasing it later. # Action What To Look For 1 Open RR. Navigate to the In Transit module → Orders page. 2 Search by ST or OT number. Use whichever you have at hand — either side of the pair finds the same row. 3 Check TranQty and TranAmt. Both should be zero if the transfer is fully complete. If only TranQty is zero but TranAmt isn’t, you have a cost variance — flag it (see below). 4 If both are zero, you’re done. The pair has cleared cleanly. No further action needed. When to Flag Something Three situations warrant raising a hand to your inventory accountant or supervisor before period close. Each one means “this pair will probably never clear by itself.” Signal What It Likely Means Who to Tell TranQty matches, TranAmt doesn’t. The ST cost and OT cost are different — usually a frozen-cost difference between branches. The pair will never clear without a JE. Inventory accountant. Partial receipt sitting open for more than a few days. Either short-shipped, items damaged, or the receiver didn’t complete the receipt. The pair won’t clear until someone resolves the missing quantity. Receiving supervisor first; inventory accountant if unresolved by week-end. You shipped to the wrong OT, or received against the wrong OT. An orphaned shipment with no match, or a receipt that crosses pairs. RR will show two pairs that won’t clear individually. Inventory accountant — this typically requires JDE-side cleanup that warehouse staff shouldn’t self-correct. Try this now If you have RR access and you’ve shipped or received any transfer this week, look it up in the Orders page using its ST or OT number. Confirm whether TranQty and TranAmt are zero. If both are zero, you’ve seen what success looks like. If they’re not zero and shouldn’t be (the receipt should have posted by now), that’s already actionable information — either there’s a real receiving issue, or the import hasn’t caught up yet (recent activity may take until tomorrow morning to appear). What Not to Do A short list, mostly to prevent well-intentioned over-correction: Don’t make corrective receipts in JDE without a paper trail. If a receipt was missed, post it through normal procedure with proper documentation. Improvised “cleanup” receipts make inventory accountants’ lives hard a month later. Don’t apply exclusions in RR. Exclusions are an inventory-accountant tool. Even if you have access to the Edit Note popup, leave the exclusion checkbox alone unless the inventory accountant explicitly asks you to act. Don’t adjust costs to make a variance disappear. Cost variances mean something — usually a frozen cost out of date on one branch. Hiding the variance hides the configuration issue that caused it. See Common Mistakes for the operational errors that cause the most In-Transit variances and how to prevent them.

Common Mistakes The top operational errors that cause In-Transit variances. Most of these can be prevented with attention at the moment of shipping or receipt — far easier than fixing them after the period closes. For warehouse personnel. Inventory accountants should also read this so they can recognize the patterns when investigating later. Multiple Orders for the Same Item The most common cause of unclearable variances. Two transfer orders move the same item from the same source branch to the same destination branch, around the same time. The receiver, in good faith, receives the full quantity against just one of the OTs — leaving the other OT with no receipt activity at all. What you see in RR: two pairs for the same item. One pair has TranQty < 0 (over-received) and TranAmt that doesn’t reconcile. The other pair has TranQty > 0 (still “in transit”) even though the goods physically arrived weeks ago. Neither pair will clear without intervention. How to prevent: when receiving, scan the OT number on the paperwork against what you’re receipting. Don’t default to the most-recent OT. If two OTs exist for the same item from the same source, both need quantity allocated — do not over-receipt one to clear the other. Receiving Against the Wrong OT A close cousin of the above, but different mechanism. The receiver records the receipt against an OT that doesn’t correspond to the actual goods that arrived. This typically happens when an OT is closed early or when paperwork mismatches. What you see in RR: the wrong-OT pair shows TranQty < 0 (received more than was shipped against this pair); the right-OT pair stays open indefinitely. Two unresolvable pairs from one mistake. How to prevent: the OT number on the receiving paperwork must match the OT being receipted against in the JDE screen. Treat any mismatch as a stop signal — raise it before completing the receipt, not after. Partial Shipments Left Open Goods are short-shipped (broken, missing, returned to stock at the source). The shipper doesn’t close out the ST after the partial shipment, so RapidReconciler still expects more goods to arrive at the destination — even though no more are coming. What you see in RR: a pair where TranQty > 0 indefinitely. Looks like “in transit” but is actually “abandoned.” How to prevent: short shipments must be closed in JDE. The remaining quantity needs to be returned to source-branch inventory through the proper transaction. Leaving the ST open isn’t a clean record-keeping shortcut — it creates a permanent open pair that someone has to investigate. Cost Differences Between Branches The ST sales price is calculated using the source branch’s cost; the OT purchase cost is calculated using the destination branch’s cost. If those costs differ — common when frozen costs are out of date on one branch or item costs were recently rolled on only one branch — the dollars don’t match even when the quantities do. What you see in RR: TranQty = 0 (full quantity received), but TranAmt ≠ 0 (residual dollars sitting in the In-Transit account). This is not a warehouse error. It’s a configuration issue with the cost setup. Flag it to your inventory accountant — they’ll either address it through a standard cost roll, post a JE, or apply an exclusion depending on materiality. Do not change costs in JDE to make the variance go away. UOM Mismatches The ST is created in one unit of measure (cases) and the receipt is processed in another (each), without RapidReconciler being able to correctly resolve the conversion. Less common than the above but particularly painful when it happens because the variance can be very large. What you see in RR: a pair where TranQty looks wildly wrong — orders of magnitude different from what physically moved. How to prevent: standardize on one UOM for transfer documentation per item. If both UOMs are valid in your environment, the JDE administrator needs to ensure UOM conversion factors are correctly configured for the affected items. Quick Reference Mistake RR Symptom Fix Receipted full qty against one of two OTs Two pairs, one over, one open Re-allocate receipt to correct OT in JDE. Receipted against wrong OT Two unresolvable pairs from one event Reverse and re-receipt in JDE; inventory accountant may need to apply exclusions. Partial shipment, ST left open Pair with TranQty > 0 indefinitely Close ST in JDE; return short qty to source inventory. Cost difference between branches TranQty = 0 but TranAmt ≠ 0 Inventory accountant decides: cost roll, JE, or exclusion. Not a warehouse fix. UOM mismatch TranQty wildly off scale JDE administrator fixes UOM configuration; inventory accountant may exclude historical pairs.

Your First Week A reading roadmap for week one as an inventory accountant taking on In-Transit reconciliation. The order is procedure-first: open the Walkthrough, follow it, and dip into the Reference Guide only when something stops making sense. Reading reference cover-to-cover before doing anything is a slower path to competence. For inventory accountants. Warehouse-only readers can skip ahead to the Self-Check. Read These — In This Order # Document Why Now 1 In Transit Reconciliation Walkthrough — Process at a Glance + Steps 1 & 2 The Process at a Glance flowchart is your mental model for the whole job. Steps 1 (verify system health) and 2 (review and triage open orders) cover what you do every time you open the application. 2 ST/OT Transfer Order Exclusion Guide Read all of it. The exclusion mechanism is genuinely RapidReconciler-specific — nothing in JDE prepares you for it. Pay particular attention to the journal entry guidance and the unexclude/re-exclude procedure. 3 Walkthrough — Steps 3 & 4 Step 3 (read the Reconciliation page) and Step 4 (resolve each variance source) are where you start working real cases. By now the exclusion guide context will help you interpret what you see. 4 Transfer Order Reference Guide Concept material. Read on demand: when the Walkthrough mentions ST/OT mechanics, related order linkage, or document type configuration and you want more depth, jump here. Defer These Excellent docs but unhelpful in week one. Bookmark them for when you need them. Document Read When Walkthrough — Steps 5, 6, 7 When your first period-end close is on the calendar (covered in Your First Period-End Close ). Walkthrough — Reference (Pages, Variances, Reports, Tools, Troubleshooting) On demand. Each Reference topic answers a specific question (“what does the As-Of page show?”, “how do I read Integrity Report 4?”). Look them up when the question arises. Common Mistakes (the warehouse topic) Skim now if you have time — the patterns help you investigate variances faster. The doc is targeted at warehouse personnel but the diagnostic value is high for inventory accountants too. Try this now Open the Orders page in RR for a closed prior period. Pick one open pair and try to figure out, just from what you can see, which of the three states (cleared, open, variance) it’s in — and what kind of pair it is (matched cleanly, cost variance, partial receipt, orphan). You don’t have to be right. The exercise is in noticing what columns you used to make the call and which ones you ignored. That tells you which Reference topic to read first when something surprises you. Stop and Ask If… Three signals that mean stop reading and find a colleague. i The In-Transit GL Balance in RR doesn’t match what JDE shows. Same logic as the other Start Here docs — the numbers come from JDE. Disagreement means your filter, period, or account selection is off. Don’t investigate variances until the GL number agrees with JDE. i You’re about to exclude a pair but the goods might still arrive. Excluding an open order is the riskiest action you can take in this module. If there’s any chance the receipt is just delayed (and not abandoned), wait. Exclusions are for known-unresolvable pairs, not late ones. i You can’t see the branches you expected. Per-module access is configured per user. If your filter list is missing a branch you should see, ask your administrator before working around it.

Your First Period-End Close The transition from monitoring In-Transit variances day-to-day to closing and posting at period end. The procedure is fully documented in the Walkthrough — Step 5 (Investigate Transactions) , Step 6 (Post Journal Entries) , and Step 7 (Run Integrity Reports) . This topic covers the things around the procedure. For inventory accountants. Warehouse-only readers: this topic doesn’t apply to your role. Two Recommendations Before You Close Live Neither of these is enforced by the application. Both are worth the time. Pair With an Experienced Reviewer for the First Close Have someone who has closed In-Transit before sit with you (or be on a call) for your first end-to-end period close. Their job is to catch the small judgment calls the documentation can’t capture — especially the exclusion-or-not decisions on real pairs, and the materiality threshold your team applies to cost variances. Walk a Closed Prior Period Before Closing Live Before your first live close, walk a closed prior period in the application without changing anything. Look at which pairs were excluded, read the audit-trail notes, see what the closing JE looked like. The reasoning behind exclusion decisions is the part that doesn’t come from JDE experience — observing how your team has handled it before is the fastest way to internalize the local standard. Pre-Close Checklist Before you post the closing journal entry, confirm: Pre-close requirements The latest import has succeeded; status indicators on the Reconciliation page are green. All pairs that should clear through normal shipping/receiving have done so — warehouse activity for the period is reflected in RR. Each remaining unresolvable pair has been reviewed and either excluded with a defensible audit-trail note or scheduled for JDE-side correction. The aggregate exclusion total on the Reconciliation page ties to the GL clearing JE you’re about to post. Integrity Report 4 (cost variances) has been reviewed; cost-difference pairs are either being addressed via standard cost roll or will be excluded. Top Three Mistakes New Inventory Accountants Make Drawn from the Walkthrough’s Best Practices and Troubleshooting sections, narrowed to the three that catch first-time closers. ! 1. Excluding open orders that are simply slow to clear. An exclusion is for pairs that will never clear — cost differences, abandoned partial shipments, mis-receipted activity. If a pair is just “in transit longer than usual,” wait. The receipt may post tomorrow. Excluding it now means having to unexclude and re-include it after the receipt arrives, which compounds work and creates audit complexity. ! 2. Not reversing the closing JE when an exclusion is later corrected. If you exclude a pair and post a JE to clear its dollar value, and then the underlying issue is resolved in JDE (the missed receipt finally posts, the cost is rolled, etc.), the exclusion needs to be reversed and the original JE needs to be reversed. Forgetting the JE reversal leaves the GL out of balance with the next period’s reconciliation. The unexclude/re-exclude procedure in the Exclusion Guide covers this. ! 3. Posting on stale data. Same as the inventory and A/P close procedures — if the import status is yellow or red, the data isn’t ready. Wait for green before producing your closing JE. The cost of a delayed close is small; the cost of a closed period that doesn’t actually agree to JDE is much larger. If Your First Close Has a Non-Zero In-Transit Variance After Posting It happens. The Audit Report tells you what was excluded, what JEs were posted, and what the balances looked like at posting time. Review with your buddy. Most causes: A pair contributed to the variance that should have been excluded but wasn’t. An exclusion was applied but the matching JE was not posted (or vice versa). The post-JE nightly refresh hadn’t completed when the variance was checked — check again the next morning. New shipping or receiving activity posted in JDE between the close and the import. None of these is a failure of the close itself. They’re learning events. Document what happened in your team’s notes — the second close benefits enormously from the first one’s post-mortem. See The Exclusion Decision for the judgment framework on when to exclude vs. wait.

The Exclusion Decision Excluding a pair removes it from the In-Transit balance and clears it from the unreconciled view. It’s the most consequential action available in the In Transit module. This topic covers when to exclude, when not to, and what happens after. For inventory accountants. Warehouse personnel should not apply exclusions; this topic explains why — and gives inventory accountants the framework for making the call. What Exclusion Does When you exclude a pair, RapidReconciler stops counting that pair’s TranAmt toward the In-Transit balance. The pair stays visible in a filtered view (excluded items don’t disappear), but it no longer affects the headline variance and no longer shows up on the unreconciled Orders page by default. What exclusion does not do: It doesn’t modify F4211 (the ST), F4311/F43121 (the OT), or any GL data. The underlying JDE records are unchanged. It doesn’t prevent future activity on the order. If a receipt is later posted in JDE against the excluded pair, the receipt flows through normally — and if it changes the pair’s state, you’ll see it via the unexclude/re-exclude procedure. It doesn’t substitute for fixing data integrity issues. If the GL is wrong, exclusion doesn’t correct it — a JE is still required. When to Exclude Exclude a pair only when it meets all three of these conditions: The pair will never clear through normal shipping/receiving. Cost difference between branches that won’t resolve via cost roll. Receipt was posted against the wrong OT and the JDE-side correction would create more issues than it solves. Partial shipment was abandoned (short-shipped, items never recovered) and source-branch inventory was already adjusted. Installation-era pair from before RR went live, which can’t be reconstructed. The GL is correct as-is, or will be corrected by the JE that accompanies the exclusion. If the GL is wrong, the JE comes first. Exclusion without a corresponding GL correction just hides the variance. The audit-trail note is defensible. What the issue is, why removing the pair from variance is acceptable, the JE or ticket reference, the date, and your initials. If you can’t write the note, you shouldn’t apply the exclusion. When Not to Exclude Situation Why Exclusion Is the Wrong Tool Pair is open but the receipt is just delayed. Wait for the next import. Most pairs that look stuck are in flight. Excluding them now creates an unexclude burden later. You’re trying to make a variance go away under deadline pressure. If you can’t articulate why the pair will never clear, you’re hiding a real issue. The variance will reappear next period in a worse form. The cost variance is large and there’s a chance the cost can be rolled. Talk to the cost accountant first. If a standard cost roll can correct the discrepancy across all affected pairs, that’s a cleaner fix than excluding each pair individually. You’re uncertain whether the pair is unresolvable or just slow. Wait one more period. The cost of waiting is one period of variance reporting; the cost of excluding prematurely is post-mortem cleanup if the receipt does arrive. The customer doesn’t have a documented materiality threshold yet. Establish the threshold first (with the controller). Without one, exclusion decisions vary by reviewer and audit reviews become contentious. The Risk of Excluding an Open Order This deserves its own emphasis because new inventory accountants often miss it. When you exclude a pair where TranQty ≠ 0 (still “in transit”), and a receipt later posts in JDE for that pair, RapidReconciler doesn’t automatically notice that the situation has changed. The exclusion stays in place; the new receipt activity sits inside the excluded pair, invisible by default. The unexclude/re-exclude procedure in the Exclusion Guide exists precisely to handle this: each period close, the inventory accountant reviews previously-excluded pairs for new activity and acts accordingly. If you exclude an open pair, you’re committing to that ongoing review — not just signing off on the current period. Quick Reference Pair Looks Like… Likely Action TranQty = 0, TranAmt = 0 Cleared. No action. TranQty > 0, recent shipment date In flight. Wait. TranQty > 0, shipment date weeks/months old Investigate. Likely partial shipment or wrong-OT issue. May exclude after investigation. TranQty = 0, TranAmt ≠ 0 (small amount) Cost variance. Likely exclude with JE if not resolvable via cost roll. TranQty = 0, TranAmt ≠ 0 (material amount) Cost variance. Investigate the cost setup with cost accountant before excluding. TranQty < 0 (over-received) Mis-receipt or duplicate-OT issue. Investigate and correct in JDE; exclusion is a last resort. See ST/OT Transfer Order Exclusion Guide for the full procedure, the unexclude/re-exclude workflow, and journal entry guidance.

Self-Check Six scenarios to test your reading. Read each one, decide what you’d do, and click Where to find this to confirm. If a scenario surprises you or you’d guess wrong, that’s the topic to re-read — not a failure. The first three scenarios apply to warehouse personnel; the last three are inventory-accountant-focused. Scenario 1 · Warehouse You shipped a transfer last Friday. It’s now Wednesday. You look up the pair in RR and TranQty > 0, TranAmt > 0. The receiving branch hasn’t entered the receipt yet. Is this normal, or should you flag it? At what point does “in flight” become “something’s wrong”? Where to find this: Your Daily Practice → What to Do When a Transfer Is Complete Scenario 2 · Warehouse You’re receiving against an OT. The paperwork shows OT 100456 but the JDE screen has OT 100455 selected by default (it’s the most recent). The items match. Receipt against the screen default, or stop and switch to the OT on the paperwork? What’s the consequence of getting this wrong? Where to find this: Common Mistakes → Receiving Against the Wrong OT Scenario 3 · Warehouse You short-shipped a transfer — only 80 of the 100 cases were available. The remaining 20 are still at the source branch. The ST is currently open with 100 cases listed. What’s the right action: leave the ST open until the remaining cases ship, or close it now and handle the 20 cases separately? Why? Where to find this: Common Mistakes → Partial Shipments Left Open Scenario 4 · Inventory Accountant A pair on the Orders page shows TranQty = 0 (full quantity received) but TranAmt = $147 (still in the In-Transit clearing account). What’s the most likely cause? Is this a candidate for exclusion, a candidate for cost roll, or something else? Where to find this: Order Pairs Explained → The Three States Scenario 5 · Inventory Accountant Period close is Friday. A pair has been sitting open for 23 days with TranQty > 0. The receiving branch supervisor confirms the goods never arrived — presumed lost in transit, no recovery expected. Apply an exclusion now to clean up the close, or post a JE first and then exclude? Or some other order? Where to find this: The Exclusion Decision → When to Exclude Scenario 6 · Inventory Accountant Three months ago, you excluded a pair because the receipt “was never going to arrive.” This morning, you notice that JDE has new receipt activity against that exact pair as of yesterday. Is the new activity contributing to the In-Transit balance? What’s the procedure to handle this, and does the original closing JE need to be reversed? Where to find this: The Exclusion Decision → The Risk of Excluding an Open Order i How to use this. The questions don’t have a score. They’re a self-test for whether the doc landed. If three or more felt uncertain, the doc didn’t land and is worth a re-read — ideally with the specific topic each scenario points to. If they all felt obvious, you’re ready to start working real cases (warehouse) or your first close (inventory accountant).

Vocabulary RapidReconciler-specific terms relevant to In-Transit work, plus a few JDE terms that get used differently here than elsewhere. Each entry is a one-line definition plus a link to the doc that explains it in depth. Exclusion (In Transit) An action that removes a single ST/OT order pair from the In-Transit balance and unreconciled view. Different from an exclusion in the PO Receipts module — same word, different mechanism, different scope. The Exclusion Decision . Exclusion (PO Receipts) — for disambiguation A line-level annotation that removes a single PO line from the RNV variance calculation. Lives in the PO Receipts module, not here. If a colleague says “exclusion” without context, ask which module they mean. Suspensions & Exclusions . Exclusion Adjust An entry shown on the In Transit As-Of view representing the dollar amount removed from the In-Transit balance via exclusions. Lets reviewers see at a glance how much of the balance is “real” in-transit activity vs. excluded. Order Pair The fundamental unit of In-Transit reconciliation. One ST sales order + one OT purchase order + one item number = one row in the In Transit module. Order Pairs Explained . OT (Other Order Type) The purchase order side of a transfer pair — created on the destination branch, records the receipt. Whether your environment uses literal “OT” or a different document type code, the role is the same. Recalc Balance button A button on the Orders page that forces RapidReconciler to recompute TranQty and TranAmt for the displayed pairs. Useful when you’ve just posted activity in JDE and want to verify the impact, but normal automatic recalculation handles most cases. Source Branch / Destination Branch The two endpoints of a transfer. Source = where goods leave from (the ST side). Destination = where goods arrive (the OT side). Costs may differ between the two branches, which is the most common cause of pure dollar variances. ST (Sales Transfer Order Type) The sales order side of a transfer pair — created on the source branch, records the shipment. Whether your environment uses literal “ST” or a different document type code, the role is the same. TranAmt Ship Amt − Rec Amt for an order pair. The dollar value still sitting in the In-Transit clearing account for that pair. The In-Transit balance is the sum of TranAmt across every open pair. TranQty Ship Qty − Rec Qty for an order pair. The quantity still in transit between source and destination. Zero means the receipt fully matched the shipment in quantity. Unexclude / Re-exclude procedure The workflow for handling a previously-excluded pair that has had new JDE activity. Remove the exclusion, review the new state, then re-apply the exclusion (or not) based on the updated facts. Transfer Order Exclusion Guide . Worked Transactions Filter A filter option on the Transactions investigation screen (Walkthrough Step 5) that lets inventory accountants hide transactions that have already been reviewed and noted. Helps focus on the unreviewed remainder during a long investigation. Back to Index for the milestones and topic table.

ST/OT Transfer Order Exclusion Guide Managing unbalanced transfers and exclusion variance in RapidReconciler so the In Transit GL account closes cleanly at period end. Overview In a perfect world, every ST/OT transfer order would ship and receive at identical quantities and amounts, leaving the In Transit clearing account with a zero balance at period end. In practice, this is rarely the case. Processing discrepancies, receiving errors, and timing differences between shipment and receipt create situations that standard JD Edwards cannot resolve once orders are closed. RapidReconciler’s Order Exclusion feature is specifically designed to handle these situations. This guide covers: What unbalanced transfer orders are and why they occur. How the Exclusion process works in RapidReconciler. How to monitor for new activity on previously excluded orders using Exclusion Variance. Best practices for maintaining a clean In Transit GL account. Topics Topic What It Covers Unbalanced Transfer Orders What an unbalanced transfer is, common causes, a worked example, the impact on reconciliation, and prevention practices. The Exclusion Process What flagging an order pair as Excluded does, the step-by-step procedure, and the benefits of using exclusion. Exclusion Variance & Audit Trail The four Excl* columns, how to interpret ExclVarQty / ExclVarAmt , the unexclude/re-exclude procedure, and the Exclusion Adjust entry in the As-Of view. Journal Entry Guidance Choosing the offsetting account, what to include in the memo, and how to handle journal entries after a re-exclusion. Period-End Workflow The recommended monthly procedure and the “exclude vs. resolve” decision framework. i Key principle. Exclusion is a reconciliation tool, not a substitute for correcting underlying data issues in JD Edwards. Where possible, resolve the root cause in JDE first. Key Takeaways Unbalanced transfer orders are common in high-volume environments and cannot be resolved through standard JD Edwards processing once both orders are closed at status 999. The Exclusion process isolates unbalanced orders from the In Transit reconciliation, surfaces the exact amount needing a journal entry, and maintains an audit trail through the Exclusion Adjust entry in As-Of details. Exclusion Variance columns ( ExclVarQty and ExclVarAmt ) protect against the risk of new activity being applied to previously excluded open orders — always review these columns during the periodic reconciliation. The unexclude/re-exclude process recalculates exclusion amounts to include all activity, ensuring the reconciliation remains accurate after new transactions occur on excluded orders. Exclusion is a reconciliation tool — it does not fix the underlying JD Edwards data. Where possible, resolve the root cause in JD Edwards before resorting to exclusion. Materiality matters — not every exclusion requires a journal entry. Establish a materiality threshold with your controller and document the policy. Small immaterial variances may be acceptable to leave without a journal entry, provided they are documented and reviewed at each period end. Related Documentation Transfer Order Reference Guide In Transit Key Concepts In Transit: Using the Application Stock Status and Trial Balance Reconciliation DMAAI Reference Guide

Unbalanced Transfer Orders — The Problem Before the Exclusion process can be applied correctly, it helps to understand what an unbalanced transfer order is, how one ends up that way, and what it does to the In Transit GL account if left alone. What Is an Unbalanced Transfer? An unbalanced transfer order occurs when the quantity or amount received does not match what was shipped, yet both the sales order (ST) and the purchase order (OT) have been fully closed at status 999 . Since In Transit is calculated as Quantity Shipped minus Quantity Received , any unbalanced transfer will remain open indefinitely in JD Edwards. There is no standard process within JD Edwards to balance these transactions once both orders are closed. Common Causes Cause Description Partial receipt applied to wrong order Multiple transfer orders exist for the same item. A receiver processes all units against one PO, leaving the other open with no receipt activity. Quantity received differs from quantity shipped A short shipment or counting error at the receiving dock results in a quantity mismatch. Both orders are eventually closed without correction. Cost variance at close The amount received differs from the amount shipped due to a cost change or pricing discrepancy, leaving a dollar balance with no quantity variance. Manual status advancement An order is advanced to status 999 manually without being fully processed, bypassing normal receipt matching. Example — Multiple Orders for the Same Item Two transfer orders are in process for the same item, each for 50 units, scheduled to ship within a week of each other. Due to delays, all 100 units are shipped together. Ship confirmations are processed correctly — 50 units on each order. At the receiving dock, the operator counts 100 units in the same box, looks up the item, and receives all 100 units against one of the two open purchase orders. ! Result. One OT order is closed correctly with 50 units shipped and 50 units received. The second OT order has 50 units shipped but zero units received — it remains open indefinitely. The In Transit GL account carries a balance for the second order with no mechanism to clear it through normal JD Edwards processing. Impact on Reconciliation Unbalanced transfer orders create a direct challenge when reconciling the In Transit GL account: The remaining balance has no corresponding open order activity to clear it. No further processing can be performed in JD Edwards once both orders are at status 999. The balance accumulates over time, making it increasingly difficult to identify which orders are genuinely in transit vs. which represent closed unbalanced transactions. At period end, the In Transit GL account will not reconcile to open order activity without accounting for these exceptions. Prevention While exclusions are sometimes unavoidable, the following practices reduce their frequency: Train receiving staff on how to identify and correctly receive against multiple open purchase orders for the same item rather than applying all units to one. Implement receiving controls that require a PO number to be scanned or entered before processing a receipt, reducing the risk of receiving against the wrong order. Monitor open transfer orders regularly so that discrepancies are caught and corrected before orders are closed at status 999. Avoid manual status advancement to 999 without confirming that all receipt and voucher activity has been properly completed. Coordinate between shipping and receiving locations on large or split shipments to ensure both sites understand which units belong to which order.

The Exclusion Process in RapidReconciler RapidReconciler’s Order Exclusion feature isolates unbalanced transfer order pairs from the In Transit reconciliation calculation so they can be cleared with a single offsetting journal entry. What the Exclusion Process Does RapidReconciler’s Order Exclusion feature allows unbalanced transfer order pairs that cannot be resolved through standard JD Edwards processing to be flagged and isolated from the In Transit reconciliation calculation. Flagging an order pair using the Exclusion feature accomplishes the following: The excluded amounts are summarized in the Variance Calculation section of the In Transit Reconciliation page, isolating the unbalanced amount as a distinct variance line item. This summarized amount identifies the exact value of the offsetting journal entry needed to bring the In Transit GL account into balance. Individual exclusions are listed in the In Transit As-Of transaction details as an “Exclusion Adjust” entry, providing a complete audit trail of all excluded order activity. Step-by-Step Exclusion Procedure Follow these steps to exclude an unbalanced transfer order pair: # Step Detail 1 Identify the unbalanced order pair Navigate to the In Transit Orders page in RapidReconciler. Review orders where both the ST and OT are at status 999 (fully closed in JD Edwards) and a remaining quantity or dollar variance exists on the order pair. 2 Verify the order cannot be resolved in JD Edwards Before excluding, confirm that there is no further processing available in JD Edwards to correct the imbalance. Orders at status 999 cannot be re-opened without reversals. 3 Flag the order pair using the Exclusion process Select the order pair on the In Transit Orders page and apply the Exclusion flag. 4 Review the updated Variance Calculation After exclusion, navigate to the In Transit Reconciliation page. The excluded amount will now appear as a distinct Exclusions variance line item in the Variance Calculation section, separate from legitimate open in-transit activity. 5 Prepare and post the offsetting journal entry in JD Edwards Use the summarized exclusion amount to prepare the appropriate offsetting journal entry to clear the unbalanced amount from the In Transit GL account. The journal entry should debit or credit the In Transit account and offset to an appropriate expense or variance account based on your organization’s policies. See Journal Entry Guidance . 6 Document the exclusion Record the order numbers, amounts, and reason for the exclusion for audit and period-end documentation purposes. Benefits of the Exclusion Process Benefit Description Identification Surfaces unbalanced transfer orders that would otherwise remain undetected within the In Transit account balance. Isolation Separates unresolvable balances from legitimate open in-transit activity, making the reconciliation cleaner and more accurate. Correction Enables preparation of accurate offsetting journal entries to clear unbalanced amounts from the In Transit GL account. Audit Trail Individual exclusion details appear in the In Transit As-Of transaction details as “Exclusion Adjust” entries, supporting period-end documentation.

Exclusion Variance & Audit Trail Once an order pair is excluded, two things matter on an ongoing basis: making sure no new activity slips into the previously-excluded order without being noticed, and preserving an audit trail of the adjustment in the As-Of view. The Risk of Excluding an Open Order When an order pair is excluded because it cannot be resolved, a secondary risk is introduced: if the OT purchase order remains open in JD Edwards at a status below 999, it is still possible for items to be received against it at a future date — even from an unrelated shipment. If this occurs, the new receipt activity will not be reflected in RapidReconciler’s In Transit calculations because the order was previously excluded. This can result in: New transactions being omitted from the reconciliation. The In Transit GL account carrying a balance that does not match the RapidReconciler position. An understated or overstated In Transit variance at period end. Exclusion Variance Columns on the Orders Tab To address this risk, four columns are available on the Orders tab in the In Transit module: Column Description ExclQty The quantity excluded on the order. ExclAmt The dollar amount excluded on the order. ExclVarQty The exclusion quantity variance — identifies any quantity activity on a previously excluded order since it was excluded. ExclVarAmt The exclusion amount variance — identifies any dollar activity on a previously excluded order since it was excluded. How to Interpret the Exclusion Variance Columns The ExclVarQty and ExclVarAmt columns are the key fields to monitor during the periodic reconciliation review. Value Interpretation Action Required Zero No new activity has occurred on the excluded order. No action needed. Non-zero Transaction activity has occurred on a previously excluded order. Unexclude and re-exclude the order pair. Unexclude and Re-Exclude Procedure When non-zero values appear in the ExclVarQty or ExclVarAmt columns, the affected order pair must be reprocessed as follows: # Step Detail 1 Unexclude the order pair Remove the Exclusion flag from the order pair to reinstate it in the In Transit calculations. This allows the new transaction activity to be visible in the reconciliation. 2 Review the updated order activity With the exclusion removed, review the order pair to understand what new transactions occurred and whether the order can now be fully resolved in JD Edwards. 3 Re-exclude the order pair Re-apply the Exclusion flag. The exclusion quantities and amounts will be recalculated to include all activity — both the original excluded transactions and the new ones. This ensures the In Transit reconciliation reflects an accurate and complete picture. 4 Update the journal entry if needed If the new activity changes the exclusion amount, update or reverse the previously posted offsetting journal entry and post a corrected entry for the new net amount. See Journal Entry Guidance → Reversing a Journal Entry After Re-Exclusion . Exclusion Adjust in the In Transit As-Of View When orders are excluded, an “Exclusion Adjust” entry appears in the In Transit As-Of transaction details for the affected item. This entry: Represents the adjustment made by the exclusion process to the item’s in-transit balance. Is included in the As-Of calculation to ensure the period-end in-transit position is accurately stated. Provides an audit trail showing when and how much was excluded for each item. i Note. The Exclusion Adjust entry in the As-Of view is informational. It does not represent a JD Edwards transaction — it is a RapidReconciler adjustment to the reconciliation position.

Journal Entry Guidance Once an order pair has been excluded, an offsetting journal entry is required to clear the unbalanced amount from the In Transit GL account. The appropriate entry depends on why the imbalance exists. Determining the Correct Offsetting Account Scenario Debit Credit Notes Quantity shipped but never received (goods lost or written off). Expense / Write-Off account In Transit Represents the cost of goods that were never received. Quantity received against wrong order (goods actually received). In Transit Inventory Goods are physically in stock; inventory account should carry the value. Cost variance between ship and receive amounts. In Transit or Variance account In Transit or Variance account Depends on whether the variance is favorable or unfavorable and your organization’s policy. Duplicate shipment confirmation (goods not actually shipped). In Transit Inventory (Branch A) Reverses the incorrect shipment entry. ! Important. Consult your cost accountant or controller before posting any manual journal entry to the In Transit account. The appropriate offsetting account will depend on the specific circumstances of the unbalanced order and your organization’s accounting policies. Documenting the Journal Entry Every manual journal entry posted to clear an excluded order should include the following in the memo or description field: The ST order number and OT order number. The item number and quantity involved. The reason the order was excluded (e.g., “received against wrong PO,” “quantity discrepancy at close”). The date of the exclusion in RapidReconciler. Reference to the RapidReconciler exclusion for audit traceability. Reversing a Journal Entry After Re-Exclusion If new activity occurs on a previously excluded order and the unexclude/re-exclude process is performed, the original journal entry may need to be adjusted: Determine the new net exclusion amount after re-exclusion. If the amount has changed, reverse the original journal entry. Post a new journal entry for the updated net amount. Update the documentation to reflect the revised entry. See Exclusion Variance & Audit Trail → Unexclude and Re-Exclude Procedure for the steps that trigger a journal-entry revision.

Period-End Reconciliation Workflow A monthly procedure for working through exclusions cleanly, plus a decision framework for choosing between excluding an order and resolving it in JDE. Recommended Monthly Procedure # Action 1 Open the In Transit Orders page in RapidReconciler. 2 Filter for order pairs at status 999 with a remaining open quantity or amount — these are candidates for exclusion. 3 Verify each candidate cannot be resolved in JD Edwards before excluding. 4 Apply the Exclusion process to confirmed unbalanced order pairs. 5 Review the Variance Calculation section on the In Transit Reconciliation page — excluded amounts now appear as a distinct line item. 6 Prepare and post the offsetting journal entry in JD Edwards for the total exclusion amount. 7 Check the ExclVarQty and ExclVarAmt columns for all previously excluded orders — address any non-zero values using the unexclude/re-exclude process. 8 Document all exclusions and journal entries for audit purposes. Exclusion vs. Resolution — Choosing the Right Approach The exclusion process is a reconciliation tool, not a substitute for correcting underlying data issues in JD Edwards. Before excluding an order, consider the following: Question If Yes If No Can the order be corrected in JD Edwards (e.g., additional receipt, reversal, manual status change)? Correct in JD Edwards first. Proceed with exclusion. Is the order genuinely closed (status 999 on both ST and OT)? Proceed with exclusion. Investigate whether further processing is possible. Is the variance amount material enough to require a journal entry? Post journal entry after exclusion. Evaluate based on materiality thresholds. Could the open OT order attract future unrelated receipts? Monitor ExclVarQty and ExclVarAmt regularly. Standard exclusion with periodic monitoring. Period-End Checklist Before signing off on In Transit All status-999 candidates with remaining variance reviewed and either resolved in JDE or excluded. Offsetting journal entry posted for the total exclusion amount. ExclVarQty and ExclVarAmt columns reviewed for all previously excluded orders; non-zero values reprocessed via unexclude/re-exclude. Each exclusion documented with order numbers, item, amount, reason, and exclusion date. Materiality decisions documented for any variances left without a journal entry. See Journal Entry Guidance for offsetting-account selection and memo content.

In Transit Reconciliation Walkthrough This guide walks you through reconciling the In Transit clearing account in RapidReconciler from start to finish. By the end of the process you will be able to confirm the In Transit GL balance ties to open transfer order activity, identify and resolve every source of variance, post the correct journal entries in JD Edwards, and produce a complete audit trail for period-end close. Quick Reference — The Process at a Glance The reconciliation is a seven-step process. Each step builds on the previous one — confirm system health first, triage open orders, then quantify and resolve each source of variance. # Step Goal 1 Log in & verify system health Both status lights green 2 Review and triage open orders Exclude closed-but-unbalanced pairs 3 Read the Reconciliation page Identify the Out of Balance amount 4 Resolve each variance source Address every non-zero variance line 5 Investigate transaction-level variances Understand root cause of each item 6 Post journal entries and close Clear the remaining variance 7 Run integrity reports & save audit Confirm clean configuration; document the close Key principle RapidReconciler does not correct In Transit issues. All corrections happen in JD Edwards or through manual journal entries. RapidReconciler's job is to tell you exactly which orders are unbalanced, by how much, and why — so you can take the right action. Where to start If this is your first time, work through the topics in order using the sidebar — beginning with Purpose & Scope , then the Process Flow Diagram . Make sure to review Order Pairs Explained before starting the seven-step process — understanding how shipments and receipts are matched is essential to everything that follows. If you're looking up a specific step or reference, jump directly to it from the sidebar.

Purpose & Scope What this guide covers, what is being reconciled, and the principles that govern the work. What You Will Be Able to Do By the end of this process, you will be able to: Confirm the In Transit GL balance ties to open transfer order activity Identify and resolve every source of variance Post the correct journal entries in JD Edwards Produce a complete audit trail for period-end close What Is Being Reconciled When goods are shipped from Branch A to Branch B, their value sits in a clearing account (typically via DMAAI 4220 or 4245) until they are received at the destination. If the shipment and receipt do not match in both quantity and amount, a balance remains in the clearing account that must be explained and cleared. Side Source Description In Transit Balance ST/OT order pairs (Shipments − Receipts) Open transfer order activity GL Balance F0902 Account Balances Period-end balance for the In Transit clearing account Out of Balance The difference between the two What you must explain and resolve Key principle RapidReconciler does not correct In Transit issues. All corrections happen in JD Edwards or through manual journal entries. RapidReconciler's job is to tell you exactly which orders are unbalanced, by how much, and why — so you can take the right action. Note on terminology Although this guide refers to ST/OT order types, RapidReconciler detects different transfer types based on a flag in the F4211 table.

Process Flow Diagram The reconciliation is a decision-driven process. Follow the flow below — each diamond represents a check that determines the next step. Green endpoints indicate the period is reconciled; the path through the steps is the recovery loop when variances exist. BEGIN RECONCILIATION START 1 Log in & select period Verify both status lights are GREEN In Transit Validation · System Status Both lights GREEN? NO RESOLVE PRIOR period or contact admin YES 2 Review the Orders page Exclude status-999 pairs Check ExclVarQty / ExclVarAmt 3 Read Reconciliation page Quantify the Out of Balance amount Out of Balance = $0? YES Skip to Step 7 NO 4 Resolve each variance line Carry Forward · GL Batches · End of Day Transactions · Exclusions · Manual JEs 5 Investigate Transactions page Identify root cause of each item 6 Post JEs in JD Edwards Wait for next nightly refresh Loop back to Step 4 if needed 7 Run Integrity Reports Save the Audit Report Reports 1–4 · period-end documentation PERIOD RECONCILED END How to read the diagram Yellow diamonds are decision points. The path through the seven blue-numbered steps is the standard route when variances exist; the shortcut on the right (yellow diamond → Step 7) is the path when the period is already in balance.

Before You Begin Confirm prerequisites, internalize the critical rules, and adopt the right mindset before opening the application. Prerequisites Item Requirement RapidReconciler access Login credentials provided by your administrator Permissions Access to the In Transit module and applicable companies JD Edwards access Required to investigate and correct issues identified in RapidReconciler Background knowledge Familiarity with ST/OT order types, DMAAI configuration, and the In Transit clearing account Critical Rules to Remember Rule 1 — Status lights Both status lights must be GREEN before making any GL adjustments. Rule 2 — Refresh cycle Data refreshes nightly. JD Edwards transactions entered today won't appear until tomorrow's refresh. Rule 3 — Read-only RapidReconciler is read-only. Every correction is made in JD Edwards. Rule 4 — Orders page The Orders page always shows the current position — changing the period selector does not change what it displays. Mindset for Success Do this often, not just at month-end. A weekly walk-through of the Orders page prevents period-end surprises. Document as you go. Notes on transactions become the audit trail. Always investigate before excluding. An exclusion you regret is harder to undo than one you delayed.

Order Pairs Explained The order pair is the core concept behind the Orders page. The Orders page matches each shipment to its corresponding receipt and presents them as a single row — that row is the order pair. Understanding how this matching works is essential before starting the seven-step process, because exclusions, variances, and the In Transit balance itself all flow from it. Scope Order pairs are specific to the Orders page . The Reconciliation, Transactions, and As-Of pages each present a different view of the data and are not organized by order pair. What Is an Order Pair? A transfer of goods between two branches in JD Edwards is recorded as two related orders : Shipping Side ST Order Transfer sales order created at the source branch when goods are shipped out. Receiving Side OT Order Transfer purchase order created at the destination branch when goods are received in. An order pair is the single, summarized view of one ST + one OT working together at the item number level . The same physical transfer of an item appears as one row in the In Transit module — not two — because both sides of the transfer are matched into a single record. How the Pairing Works The Orders page matches each shipment to its corresponding receipt using related order data stored on the sales order. When a transfer order is created in JD Edwards, the F4211 record carries a reference back to the originating purchase order, and RapidReconciler uses this link to bring the two sides together. The match key RapidReconciler pairs the ST sales order to the OT purchase order using the related order fields on F4211. The pair is then summarized at the item number level — one ST + one OT + one item = one row. The Two Sides of an Order Pair The diagram below summarizes how the two JD Edwards orders are joined into a single In Transit row. RapidReconciler reads each side from the underlying JDE table, then matches them using the related order data on the F4211 sales order: Aspect ST Sales Order (shipping side) OT Purchase Order (receiving side) JD Edwards table F4211 (sales order detail) F4311 / F43121 (PO detail / receipts) Branch Source branch — where goods leave from Destination branch — where goods arrive Records The shipment The receipt Match key Related order fields on the F4211 record link the two sides together Result One ST + One OT + One Item Number = One row in the In Transit module The pair is summarized at the item number level . If a single transfer moves multiple items, each item produces its own pair — one row per ST/OT/item combination. What an Order Pair Tells You Once paired, RapidReconciler calculates the in-transit position for each order pair by subtracting receipts from shipments: Field Calculation What It Means TranQty Ship Qty − Rec Qty Quantity still in transit between the two branches TranAmt Ship Amt − Rec Amt Dollar value still sitting in the In Transit clearing account When TranQty and TranAmt are both zero, the pair is fully cleared — quantity shipped equals quantity received, and the dollar value has cleared the In Transit account. Any non-zero value means there is open transfer activity that contributes to the In Transit balance. Why This Matters for Reconciliation The In Transit balance is the sum of every open pair's TranAmt. The total at the bottom of the Orders page rolls up directly into the In Transit Balance shown on the Reconciliation page. Exclusions happen at the pair level. When you exclude an order in Step 2, you're telling RapidReconciler to stop counting that one ST/OT/item combination toward the In Transit balance. Cost variances surface at the pair level. Integrity Report 4 examines each pair to find cases where the ST sales price doesn't equal the OT purchase cost — these will never clear without action. Open pairs are the source of every variance you'll investigate. Whatever you see on the Reconciliation page can be traced back to one or more open pairs on the Orders page — which is why Step 2 (triaging the Orders page) comes before any other corrective action. Note on terminology Although this guide refers to ST/OT order types, RapidReconciler detects different transfer types based on a flag in the F4211 table. The pairing logic is the same regardless of the specific order type codes used in your environment.

section_title: Log In and Verify System Health
body: 1 Step One Log In and Verify System Health Goal Confirm the system is ready for reconciliation work. Reconciliation Page Header Both status indicators showing green — the system is ready for reconciliation work. Procedure Log in to RapidReconciler at https://rapidreconciler.getgsi.com . Navigate to the In Transit module. The Reconciliation page opens by default. Use the period selector (top right) to choose the period being reconciled. Apply company and account filters as needed (Company → Business Unit → Object → Subsidiary). Verify the two status indicators: Indicator Green Means If Red In Transit Validation The carry-forward from prior period is accurate Hover for details; resolve the prior period before proceeding System Status The JD Edwards import completed successfully Hover for details; contact your administrator Yellow status If the System Status light is flashing yellow , the import is in progress. Wait for it to finish before continuing. Stop condition Do not proceed to Step 2 if either light is red. Fixing the underlying issue first prevents wasted work.

2 Step Two Review and Triage Open Orders Goal Make sure the Orders page lists only what is truly in transit. Everything else must be excluded. Filter Buttons on the Orders Page At the top of the Orders grid are three buttons that automatically filter the result set. Each one isolates a specific class of order pair that needs attention. Orders Page The three filter buttons at the top of the In Transit Orders grid. Receipts without Shipments If the age of the receipt is large, this is more than likely a timing issue with the start date — the order should probably be excluded. If the date is more recent, contact the shipping branch to investigate a potential missing transaction. Items in Transit with Closed PO Orders where the lowest next status on any line is 999 and there is a non-zero quantity in transit. These orders need to be investigated as the quantity shipped does not agree with the quantity received. Zero Quantity in Transit Orders where the ship quantity matches the receipt quantity but there is an amount mismatch that must be excluded. Any exclusion amount adds to the Exclusion variance source and will be cleared during the close. The goal There should be no data showing for any of the three filters. Each filter that returns rows is a list of orders that need triage before the period can be reconciled cleanly. Procedure Navigate to the Orders page . Review every order pair. For each one, decide its disposition: Situation Action Both ST and OT at status 999 with remaining balance Exclude — no further JD Edwards activity is possible OT not yet received or partially received; more receipts expected Leave open — legitimately in transit Partial receipt; remaining quantity unclear Investigate with the warehouse before excluding Cost variance only; quantity is zero Exclude and post a JE for the dollar variance Before excluding, research each order in JD Edwards to confirm no further activity will occur. Check the ExclVarQty (Excluded Quantity Variance) and ExclVarAmt (Excluded Amount Variance) columns. Any non-zero value means new transactions have hit a previously excluded order — you must unexclude and re-exclude to recalculate. Note the total Exclusions amount at the bottom of the page. This becomes your offsetting journal entry amount in Step 6. What is an exclusion? Excluding an order pair tells RapidReconciler to stop counting it toward the In Transit balance. It does not change anything in JD Edwards — the GL balance still needs an offsetting journal entry. Order pairs must be investigated in JD Edwards before they are excluded. Although an exclusion can be reversed at any time by unexcluding the order, exclusions made without first confirming the underlying cause can mask real issues — missing transactions, DMAAI configuration problems, or transfer cost variances that need to be corrected at the source. Order Pair Columns to Know Column Meaning From / To Shipping and receiving branch plants TranQty In-transit quantity (Ship Qty − Rec Qty) TranAmt In-transit amount (Ship Amt − Rec Amt) Ship Sts / Rec Sts Current status of the ST and OT orders ExclQty / ExclAmt Quantity and amount previously excluded ExclVarQty / ExclVarAmt New activity on previously excluded orders — review every period The Recalc Balance Button RapidReconciler's data refreshes nightly, but exclusion changes you make on the Orders page do not need to wait for the next refresh cycle. Above the Orders grid (visible in the screenshot at the top of this page) is a Recalc Balance button. When to use it What it does After excluding or unexcluding one or more order pairs Immediately updates the In Transit Balance and variance calculations on the Reconciliation page so you can see the impact of your triage work without waiting for the nightly refresh. Workflow Complete all your exclusion decisions for the period first, then click Recalc Balance once. There is no need to recalc after every individual exclusion — batching them is faster and produces the same result. Reminder Recalc Balance only updates the calculations driven by Orders page activity (exclusions and the In Transit Balance). Journal entries posted in JD Edwards still require the next nightly refresh before they appear in RapidReconciler.

3 Step Three Read the Reconciliation Page Goal Quantify the Out of Balance amount and decide what to investigate. Reconciliation Page The Valuation panel (top) and Variance Calculation panel (bottom). Out of Balance ties to the Unreconciled Variance — both shown in orange. Procedure Return to the Reconciliation page . Look at the Valuation section : GL Balance F0902 Period-end balance of the In Transit clearing account In Transit Balance ST/OT Pairs Calculated from open transfer activity (Shipments − Receipts) Out of Balance GL − In Transit What needs to be explained and resolved Decision If Out of Balance = $0 , skip ahead to Step 7 — you are reconciled. If Out of Balance ≠ $0 , continue to Step 4 to address each variance source.

4 Step Four Resolve Each Variance Source Goal Walk down the Variance Calculation section and address every non-zero line. The sum of all lines equals the Out of Balance amount. Variance Lines at a Glance Variance Line Must Be Zero to Close? Resolution Carry Forward Investigate Resolve in prior period or roll into current JE GL Batches Yes Work with finance to post all open batches End of Day Yes Confirm Sales Update (R42800) ran for transfer orders Transactions Investigate Drill in via Step 5 and prepare offsetting JEs Exclusions Prepare JE Post journal entry for the excluded amount Manual Journal Entries Informational Confirm entries are correctly reflected Carry Forward The unresolved out-of-balance amount from the prior period. Resolve by: Returning to the prior period to investigate, or — if the prior period is closed — including the amount in the current period's manual journal entry. Document the reason. GL Batches Entries in F0911 where the posted code is not "P" (not yet posted to the GL). Resolve by: Working with finance to post the outstanding batches. A bell icon flags batches more than 2 days old — prioritize these. If a batch header is missing, run JD Edwards' "Missing Batch Header" report. Required GL Batches must be zero before performing closing activities. End of Day Transfer sales orders that have been ship confirmed but not yet processed through Sales Update (R42800/P42800). Resolve by: Confirming the Sales Update version configured for transfer orders is scheduled and completing successfully. The In Transit Sales Update version must have the A/R interface turned off . A bell icon flags orders more than 2 days old. Required End of Day must be zero before closing. Until Sales Update runs, the shipment has no batch number or GL date. Transactions Documents where the order lines (F4211 sales orders or F4311 purchase orders) do not match the GL (F0911). Common causes: DMAAI mismatches, fiscal period differences, account number discrepancies. Resolve by: Going to the Transactions page (Step 5) to investigate each item. The corrective action is always an offsetting JE in JD Edwards plus a note in RapidReconciler. Exclusions The "leftover" balance from order pairs you excluded in Step 2. Resolve by: Posting a journal entry in JD Edwards — debit or credit the In Transit account and offset to an appropriate variance or expense account per your organization's policy. Manual Journal Entries Manual entries posted directly to the In Transit GL account. Informational only — confirms they are reflected in the variance calculation. Example Variance Calculation Variance Source Amount Status Carry Forward ($750.10) Investigate prior period GL Batches $0.00 ✓ Clear End of Day $0.00 ✓ Clear Transactions $0.00 ✓ Clear Exclusions ($23.31) Post journal entry Manual Journal Entries $0.00 ✓ Clear Unreconciled Variance ($773.41) Total requiring resolution

5 Step Five Investigate Transaction-Level Variances Goal Determine the root cause of every transaction variance and prepare corrective journal entries. Transactions Page The Edit Note button and the Worked Transactions filter sit in the Details bar above the grid. Procedure Navigate to the Transactions page . Note that transactions differing by less than 1 monetary unit are filtered by default but still counted in totals (administrator can adjust the tolerance). For each row, click the + icon on the left to open the Transaction Detail report. The report has three sections: Section Contents 1 — F0911 GL All F0911 rows for the company, document type, and document number 2 — Order Lines For sales shipments, the F4211 sales order lines. For PO receipts, the F4311 purchase order lines. 3 — DMAAIs All DMAAI entries for each GL class code (first row = model table) Analysis Tips Verify company number, account number, and period ending date match across Sections 1 and 2. If account numbers differ, consult the DMAAI setup in Section 3. Refer to the DMAAI Reference Guide for configuration help. Determine the root cause: DMAAI mismatch , period mismatch , or account mismatch . Prepare an offsetting journal entry in JD Edwards for each variance. Select the row on the Transactions page and click the Edit Note button to document the corrective action and (optionally) mark the row as worked. The Edit Note Popup Clicking Edit Note opens a popup that combines two actions in a single dialog: Field Purpose Worked checkbox Marks the transaction as worked. Worked rows can be filtered off the grid using the Worked Transactions dropdown — but totals are not affected. Note input Free-text field for documenting the corrective action. Notes appear on the period-end audit report. Best practice Include your name and date in every note. Notes appear on the period-end audit report and become the audit trail for how each variance was resolved. The Worked Transactions Filter The Worked Transactions dropdown above the grid controls which rows are visible. It has three values: Value What It Shows No Only transactions that have not yet been marked as worked. This is the working view during reconciliation — it leaves only what still needs attention. Yes Only transactions that have been marked as worked. Useful for reviewing what's been documented and confirming notes are complete before close. All Every transaction, regardless of worked status. Use this when reconciling totals or auditing. Important The Worked Transactions filter is a view filter only . It changes which rows appear on the grid but does not change the variance totals on the Reconciliation page. Marking a transaction worked does not resolve it — the underlying journal entry must still be posted in JD Edwards.

6 Step Six Post Journal Entries and Close Goal Clear the remaining variance and confirm the period reconciles. Pre-Close Checklist Confirm before posting GL Batches = $0 End of Day = $0 All transaction variances reviewed and notes documented Calculate the Closing Journal Entry Total Once GL Batches and End of Day are at zero, the remaining Out of Balance is composed of three resolvable variance lines. The closing journal entry is a single combined entry that totals these three values to bring the Out of Balance to zero: Closing JE Formula Carry Forward + Transactions + Exclusions The sum of all unresolved variances. Posting this entry should drive Out of Balance to $0 . Component What It Represents Carry Forward The unresolved out-of-balance amount carried over from the prior period (when the prior period is closed and cannot be returned to). Transactions The net total of all F4211 (sales order) or F4311 (purchase order) vs F0911 mismatches investigated in Step 5. Exclusions The leftover balance from order pairs excluded in Step 2. Why a single entry Combining the three lines into one journal entry simplifies the audit trail and ensures the period closes cleanly. The Manual Journal Entries line on the Reconciliation page will reflect the posted total once the next nightly refresh runs, and the Out of Balance should be exactly zero. Posting the Journal Entry Use This Tool When Journal Entry button On the Reconciliation page — produces an Excel report of GL and In Transit balances to support account-level entries. Suggested Entry popup (star icon, End of Day row) Generates a pre-built suggested journal entry for End of Day amounts at close. Powered by the offset accounts configured by an administrator. Manual JE in JD Edwards The actual closing entry for Carry Forward + Transactions + Exclusions is posted manually in JD Edwards. See the Transfer Order Exclusion Guide for guidance on the exclusion portion. The Suggested Entry Popup Clicking the star icon on the End of Day row in the Variance Calculation section opens the RR Suggested Entry popup. The popup is pre-built using the offset accounts configured by an administrator (see the Administrator Walkthrough → Offset Accounts) and gives you a head start on the closing journal entry. RR Suggested Entry Popup One row per company with SEQ 0 (debit/credit on the In Transit account) paired with SEQ 1 (debit/credit on the configured offset account). The Export button generates an Excel file with the entry pre-formatted. Reading the popup Column Description CompanyNumber The company the entry is for. Multi-company environments produce multiple paired rows in a single popup. PeriodEnds The period the entry should be posted to. LongAccount The In Transit clearing GL account being cleared. SEQ Sequence within the entry. SEQ 0 is the In Transit side; SEQ 1 is the offset side. The two amounts net to zero per pair. JE_Account The GL account for that line. SEQ 0 always uses the In Transit account itself. SEQ 1 uses the offset account configured by the administrator for the matching Company / DocType / OrderType combination. JE_Amount The dollar amount for that line. Negatives clear the In Transit balance; positives post to the offset account. Working with the entry Click Export to download the entry as Excel. Review the JE_Account column. If any rows show a generic placeholder (such as COGS , Tolerance Adjust , or TBD ), replace them with the correct GL accounts per your internal procedures. The freeform field on the offset accounts setup intentionally allows generic labels — they are placeholders waiting for resolution at close. Copy the two rightmost columns ( JE_Account and JE_Amount ) into your JD Edwards journal entry template. Post the JE in JDE. End of Day only — close-only tool The Suggested Entry popup is for the End of Day variance source only. There is no equivalent for the Transactions row in this module — those variances are handled through individual Edit Note entries and a manual JE. Also do not use the Suggested Entry popup mid-period — the offset accounts are configured to clear what's open at close, and using it before close can produce an incomplete entry. Confirm Reconciliation After posting in JD Edwards: Wait for the next nightly refresh — RapidReconciler does not see your JE until then. Return to the Reconciliation page. Confirm Out of Balance = $0 . If a residual variance remains, return to Step 4 and identify which line moved.

section_title: Run Integrity Reports and Save the Audit Report
body: 7 Step Seven Run Integrity Reports and Save the Audit Report Goal Verify clean configuration and produce permanent documentation. Run the Four Integrity Reports Report Name Purpose 1 Model AAI Table Verify all GL class codes have correct account assignments 2 Missing GL Classes Add missing GL class codes to the transit DMAAI table 4310 3 Order Exclusions Confirm all exclusions are correct and accounted for 4 Transfer Cost Variances Investigate any orders with non-zero UnitPrVar — most common cause of unresolved In Transit balances Save the Audit Report Click the Audit Report button. Save the output (Excel or PDF) to a dedicated period-end folder. Important Save the Audit Report before any data purge. Detail data may be removed during a purge and cannot be recreated. What the Audit Report Contains Section Contents Accounts Summary Valuation and variance summary per account Unposted GL Batches Details of any remaining unposted batches End of Day Sales orders awaiting Sales Update Manual Journal Entries All manual entries to the account Variances Transaction variances with user-entered notes Perpetual Details Item balances and values at period end You're done The period is reconciled and documented.

section_title: Reference — Key Pages and What They Show
body: Reference — Key Pages and What They Show The four primary pages in the In Transit module and how they behave with the period selector. Page Shows Period Behavior Reconciliation Valuation, variance calculation, and overall close status Honors selected period Orders Open ST/OT order pairs with a remaining quantity or amount Always current position regardless of period Transactions F4211 / F4311 vs F0911 mismatches at the document level Honors selected period As-Of In-transit position as of any historical period end Calculated backwards from the Orders page totals About the As-Of Page The As-Of page reconstructs what was in transit at any prior period end by walking backward from the current Orders page totals. The result is a complete, item-level snapshot showing what goods were sitting in the In Transit clearing account at the close of the selected period — useful for audit support, period comparisons, and month-end review. In Transit > As-Of The As-Of page with one row expanded to reveal its transaction history. Item identifiers and descriptions are generic placeholders for documentation purposes. Behavior The total will always match the Reconciliation page total for the same period. It will only match the Orders page total if the current period is selected. Calculations are dynamic — backdated activity changes prior As-Of positions. "Exclusion Adjust" entries in detail are RapidReconciler adjustments, not JD Edwards transactions. Expanding a Row — The In Transit Cardex Clicking the + icon at the left of any row expands an inline sub-grid that acts as a cardex for In Transit — the full transaction history for that single item, walked backward in date sequence from the selected period end. This is the most powerful feature of the As-Of page. The first row of the expansion shows the period-end balance ("End bal"). Each subsequent row is a transaction that affected the in-transit position for that item, ordered most-recent-first. Together, they tell the story of how that item's in-transit balance was built up over time. Sub-grid columns Column Description company JD Edwards company number associated with the transaction. longaccount The In Transit clearing GL account. branch The branch plant on the transaction (typically the shipping or receiving branch). itemnumber The item being transferred — same item across all rows of the expansion. lot Lot number, when the item is lot-controlled. glclass The GL class code on the transaction, used to map into the DMAAI tables. period end date The period the transaction is recorded against. creation date The date the transaction was created in JD Edwards. Differences between this and the period end date indicate backdated activity. comments Notes on the row — for example, the first row of the expansion is labeled "End bal" to indicate the period-ending balance. Source data The transaction detail in the expansion is sourced from sales and purchase orders (F4211 / F4311) — not the item ledger (F4111). This is consistent with how the rest of the In Transit module operates, since transfers are recorded via ST/OT order pairs rather than direct cardex entries. Exporting the As-Of Position The As-Of page — including expanded rows — can be exported to Excel for offline analysis, audit packages, or comparison against external reports. The exported file contains more detail than the in-app expansion shows on screen, with several additional columns useful for deeper investigation. Excel Export of the As-Of Cardex The full export for a single item in transit. The first row is the period-end balance ("End bal"), the last row is the beginning balance ("Beg bal"), and every row between is a transaction that affected the in-transit position. Item identifiers and accounts are generic placeholders for documentation purposes. Additional columns in the export Beyond the columns visible in the in-app expansion, the Excel export includes several columns that support deeper analysis: Column Description runqty Running quantity. The cumulative in-transit quantity for the item as of each row, walked backward from the period-end balance. runamt Running amount. The cumulative in-transit dollar value for the item, walked backward in the same way. dt Document type code on the transaction (for example, TR for transfer receipts, OW for transfer shipments). doc JD Edwards document number for the transaction. qty Transaction quantity — the quantity moved by this single transaction (positive for receipts, negative for shipments). uom Unit of measure for the transaction quantity. val Transaction value — the dollar amount of this single transaction. ordnum Order number — the JDE order this transaction belongs to (the ST or OT order number). ordtype Order type for ordnum — typically ST (transfer sales) or OT (transfer purchase). relnum Related order number — the matched pair on the other side of the transfer. reltype Related order type for relnum . Why the running columns matter The runqty and runamt columns make it easy to spot the exact moment an item went in or out of an unbalanced state. Sorting or scanning the column reveals when the in-transit balance jumped, plateaued, or began drifting — without having to manually add up individual transactions. Worked Example When the cardex tells the real story The export above is a real-world example. The item shows an End bal runamt of -366.34 , which an analyst would naturally want to clear before period-end close. The expansion includes an Exclusion Adjust row, which is the most visually distinctive entry in the cardex — and a tempting first place to look. The intuitive (but wrong) read Looking at the expansion, the eye is drawn to the Exclusion Adjust row at 2015-10-03 . The natural conclusion: someone excluded an order pair, the exclusion is responsible for the leftover variance, and reversing it would bring End bal back to zero. What the math actually shows Walking the chronology forward from Beg bal (the bottom of the export) and summing every val , the running balance arrives at exactly -366.34 by the time we reach End bal. So the dollar variance is real — but here's the catch: Row periodends val Effect Exclusion Adjust (ST 24473 / OT 23868) 2015-10-03 0.00 No dollar impact The Exclusion Adjust has val = 0.00 . Reversing it would not change End bal at all. The exclusion is a marker, not a debit or credit. Furthermore, the order pair the exclusion applies to (ST 24473 / OT 23868) is fully balanced when its three rows are summed: +956.02 shipment + 0.00 receipt + -956.02 shipment = $0.00 . There was nothing wrong with that pair — the exclusion was a misdiagnosis. The real culprit Grouping all transactions by order pair (using the ordnum and relnum columns) reveals exactly one pair that does not net to zero: Order Pair Side Branch qty val OT 23885 / ST 24491 OT receipt (IDM) — -480 -366.34 ST shipment (CBT) — +480 0.00 Net 0 -366.34 The quantities net to zero (480 in, 480 out — the goods actually moved correctly), but the dollars don't : the receipt posted at $366.34 while the shipment posted at $0.00. This is a classic UnitPrVar — a transfer cost variance arising when the ST sales price doesn't equal the OT purchase cost.
