What you should do Always pick the most recently closed period, not the current open one — reconciliation is a backward-looking exercise. If you don't see the period you want, scroll the dropdown — older periods may be below the visible range. Changing the period reloads the page data; if numbers look wrong after a change, give the page a moment to refresh before investigating. Also called period selector, period end, period dropdown, fiscal period, change period, last period

Account Filter Where you’ll see it Left side of the Reconciliation page, four columns. What it does Filters the displayed reconciliation to a specific account, organized by JD Edwards account hierarchy: Company → Business Unit → Object → Subsidiary. What you should do Pick from left to right — selecting a Company narrows the Business Unit list, and so on. You can stop at any level: leaving Subsidiary blank includes all subsidiaries of the selected Object account. If your filter selection produces no rows, double-check the period selector — the account may have no activity in that period. Also called account filter, four columns, company business unit object subsidiary, JDE account picker

Valuation Section Where you’ll see it Reconciliation page, after the Account Filter. What it tells you Three numbers that frame the entire reconciliation: GL Balance, Perpetual Balance, and Out of Balance (the difference). States GL Balance — The dollar value in the General Ledger for the filtered account at period end. Perpetual Balance — The dollar value calculated from the perpetual inventory records for the same account at the same period. Out of Balance — The variance between the two. Zero (or near zero) means the account is reconciled. What you should do A non-zero Out of Balance is your starting point — it's the dollar amount you need to explain via the Variance Calculation section below. The GL Balance figure should match what you see in JDE for the same account and period. If it doesn't, something is wrong with the import (check the System Status light). Also called GL balance, perpetual balance, out of balance, variance, valuation summary, headline numbers

Variance Calculation Section Where you’ll see it Reconciliation page, below the Valuation Section. What it does Six lines, one per known variance source. Together they decompose the Out of Balance figure into its causes. What you should do Focus on the non-zero lines — those are where the variance is actually coming from. Click into each non-zero line to drill down to the documents driving it. A small residual after explaining the major lines is normal (rounding, immaterial timing). A large unexplained residual means a variance source not yet captured by the calculation — escalate to GSI. Also called variance breakdown, six lines, variance sources, drill down, decomposition

Out of Balance History Graph Where you’ll see it Reconciliation page, lower section. What it does A line chart showing the Out of Balance figure across the last 14 closed periods. What you should do Use it as a sanity check — is this period's variance an anomaly or part of a trend? A growing variance over time means a structural problem (DMAAI misconfiguration, unposted batches accumulating). Address the root cause; don't just clear this period. A spike in a single period usually means a one-time event (manual journal entry, reversed receipt out of sequence). Investigate that period specifically. Also called history chart, trend chart, 14 periods, variance trend, balance over time

Calculation Section Where you’ll see it PO Receipts Reconciliation page, prominent block at the top. What it does Decomposes the total RNV out-of-balance figure into its components: Open Receipts, RNV Tax, Landed Cost, and others. What you should do Read it top-down — Open Receipts is usually the largest component. A non-zero Tax line typically means tax was added at voucher but not at receipt (or vice versa). Compare F43121 tax fields to the F0911 entries to confirm. A non-zero Landed Cost line with no expected landed-cost activity often means a PRLAND = 3 record. Suspend those rather than chasing them. Also called calculation breakdown, RNV components, open receipts, RNV tax, landed cost

PO Receipts Aging Chart Where you’ll see it PO Receipts Reconciliation page. What it does Visual breakdown of open receipts by age (current, 30, 60, 90+ days). What you should do Healthy: most balance sits in the 0–60 day buckets — those are receipts waiting on normal voucher matching. Unhealthy: significant balance in the 90+ bucket. Those are aged open receipts that need investigation: late supplier invoicing, PRLAND = 3 records, or PO's closed in JDE without proper voucher match. Click a bar to filter the order list to that age band, then work the items individually. Also called aging chart, aging breakdown, 90 days, aged receipts, old receipts, aging buckets

Reconciled / Suspended Filters Where you’ll see it Top of the PO Receipts Orders page. What it does Toggle filters that let you focus on unreconciled orders by hiding orders that have already been suspended or reconciled. What you should do Default view (both filters off) shows everything — useful for full-list audits. Turn ON "Hide Suspended" to focus on orders still requiring action. Turn ON "Hide Reconciled" to drop already-cleared orders. For routine period-close work, both ON gives you the actionable working list. Also called orders filter, hide suspended, hide reconciled, working list, focus filter

Edit Note button Where you’ll see it Line Analysis page, action area. What it does Single point of control for adding, editing, or removing a note on a suspended or excluded item. What you should do Always add a note when you suspend an order — future you (or the next reconciler) needs to know why . Notes are searchable in the Orders page. A consistent note vocabulary ("PRLAND=3", "manual voucher", "aged - awaiting credit") makes audit reviews much faster. Editing or removing a note doesn't un-suspend an order — that's a separate action via the Suspended checkbox. Also called note button, edit note, add note, suspension note, comment

Document button Where you’ll see it Line Analysis page, action area. What it does Opens a side-by-side comparison of the F43121 (PO receiver) record against the F0911 (GL) record for the same document. What you should do Use it when a specific PO has a small variance you can't explain — it usually exposes penny-rounding, tax-explanation-code differences, or out-of-sequence postings. If the F43121 and F0911 amounts match exactly but the order still shows variance, the issue is on a different line of the same document. Check sibling lines. Also called document button, F43121 F0911 comparison, side by side, line compare

Suspension Feature Where you’ll see it Available on every order in the Orders page (Suspended checkbox). What it does Removes an order from the active reconciliation total without modifying the underlying JD Edwards data. What you should do Suspend orders that genuinely don't belong in the active RNV picture: PRLAND = 3 landed costs, PO's at status 999 with no further activity possible, manual GL clearances. Suspension is reversible — un-suspending puts the order back in the active total. Use this if new activity posts to a previously-suspended order. Always pair a suspension with a note (Edit Note button) explaining why. See the Suspension & Exclusion guide for the full decision tree. Also called suspend, suspension, remove from total, exclusion, suspended orders

New User button Where you’ll see it Top right corner of the Users page (Admin module). What it does Opens the New User Detail panel where you create a user record. What you should do Click it when you need to add a new user. Have the user's name, email address, and intended rights (which modules, which databases) ready before you click — partial records can't be saved. If the button is missing or grayed out, your account doesn't have user-management rights. Contact your RR Administrator. Also called add user, create user, +User, new user

Edit user details icon Where you’ll see it Center of the Options column on the Users page. Icon shows a paper with a pencil. What it does Opens the User Detail panel for editing the selected user's name, email, and basic info. What you should do Use it for routine updates — name change after marriage, email address change after a domain migration. Edits are immediate and live — there's no draft or approval step. Also called paper pencil icon, edit details, edit user, modify user, pencil icon

Edit user rights icon Where you’ll see it Left of the Options column on the Users page. Icon is a padlock. What it does Opens the user-rights editor where you grant or revoke access to specific modules and databases. What you should do Use it when a user changes roles or needs access to a new module. Rights changes take effect on the user's next login — they may need to log out and back in to see the change. Also called lock icon, padlock, user rights, user permissions, access, grant access

Delete user icon Where you’ll see it Right of the Options column on the Users page. Icon is a trash can. What it does Removes the user from the system. What you should do Prefer setting Active = No to outright deletion. Deactivation preserves history (suspensions, notes, audit trail) attributable to that user; deletion may remove some of that linkage. Use deletion only for accounts that were created in error and have no activity attached. Deletion cannot be undone — the user record is gone, not just hidden. Also called trash can, delete user, remove user, deactivate

Authorized Tabs Where you’ll see it On the user-rights editor (opened via the lock icon). What it does Four checkboxes that control which modules a user can access: Inventory, In Transit, PO Receipts, Admin. What you should do Grant only what each user needs. Most reconcilers need only one or two module tabs. "Admin" should be granted sparingly — it's the user-management tab and any other administrative function. Unchecked tabs simply don't appear in that user's Main Navigation Bar. Also called authorized tabs, module access, tab permissions, inventory in transit po receipts admin
